text: Attitude: bored; Mood: indifferent. I'm looking forward to tonight as it's been a long, hard day at work, leaving me almost no time to write for you, dear readers. It's encouraging to see Tigger, now known as Akcron, stepping up and sharing her thoughts. There's not much to report today, but yesterday I attended Shakespeare on the Green. It was a beautiful play, although I had to leave before the curtain call, which was disappointing. I hope to watch a full performance of a Shakespeare play someday. But I digress. Akcron is right about the shower problems—it's a nuisance. On a lighter note, you have to admit, watching someone struggle in the shower can be quite amusing, given the expressions people make during such moments of self-inflicted torture.

The reboot of the devil may cry franchise has brought about many changes for the series, particularly for series’ protagonist, dante. While fans of the original series may be upset at the change to their beloved character, it’s certainly for the better; and this new game by ninja theory proves it. Dmc serves as the perfect point for newcomers to jump into the series. It shows the origins of an arrogant, cynical dante, and it chronicles his journey from cynicism to selflessness. Throughout, the game weaves a clear, narrative arc for the new dante, making him a compelling character to watch.image the story follows dante as he’s introduced into the corrupt world he willfully chose to ignore.

The nonlinear fluorescence emission has been widely applied for the high spatial resolution optical imaging. Here, we studied the fluorescence anomalous saturating effect of the nitrogen vacancy defect in diamond. The fluorescence reduction was observed with high power laser excitation. It increased the nonlinearity of the fluorescence emission, and changed the spatial frequency distribution of the fluorescence image. We used a differential excitation protocol to extract the high spatial frequency information. By modulating the excitation laser's power, the spatial resolution of imaging was improved approximate 1.6 times in comparison with the confocal microscopy. Due to the simplicity of the experimental setup and data processing, we expect this method can be used for improving the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling with the defects in solids.

Let's start in 1964. Bob Dylan, at just 23 years old, is reaching the peak of his career. Dubbed the voice of a generation, Dylan is producing iconic songs at an astounding rate. However, a small group of critics accuses him of plagiarizing the work of others. Fast forward to 2004, when Brian Burton, known as Danger Mouse, merges The Beatles' "White Album" with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create "The Grey Album." This remix quickly becomes an online sensation, prompting The Beatles' record company to issue numerous cease-and-desist letters, citing "unfair competition and dilution of our valuable property." "The Grey Album" stands as a notable example of remix culture.

Sensational health headlines have become a daily staple, often presenting conflicting claims that can be misleading and confusing. The disconnect between attention-grabbing headlines and the nuanced findings of the underlying medical research can be significant. To critically evaluate the credibility of such headlines, it is essential to examine the original research they are based on. To illustrate this, let's consider three hypothetical research scenarios, each corresponding to a commonly encountered type of headline. By analyzing these examples, we can develop a more informed approach to assessing the validity of health-related news stories.

In this study, we investigate the topography of the negative log-likelihood function of Gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to the non-convex nature of the problem, multiple local minima may exist, even when the mixture is well-separated. We demonstrate that all local minima share a common structural property, which provides partial identification of the component centers of the true mixture. Specifically, each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple Gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single Gaussian to multiple true components. Our findings are applicable to the scenario where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition and remain valid even when the number of components is over- or under-specified. In the case of Gaussian mixtures with three components, we achieve sharper results in terms of the scaling between the components, as well as the separation between the components.

In a surprising turn of events, Jurgen Melzer defeated second-seed Andre Agassi in the quarter-finals of the SAP Open. The Austrian's effective use of drop shots left Agassi struggling, resulting in a 6-3, 6-1 loss for the American in San Jose. Meanwhile, defending champion and top seed Andy Roddick made a comeback to overcome Sweden's Thomas Enqvist with scores of 3-6, 7-6 (8-6), 7-5. Additionally, unseeded Cyril Saulnier triumphed over fourth-seed Vincent Spadea, winning 6-2, 6-4, while Tommy Haas edged out eighth-seed Max Mirnyi with scores of 6-7 (2-7), 7-6 (7-3), 6-2. This marks the second victory for Melzer over Agassi in their three encounters. "I had a solid game plan and executed it flawlessly," Melzer commented. "Facing Andre is always challenging. I aimed to disrupt his style because he can make you chase the ball all over the court like a dog.

As a palliative care physician, I have had the privilege of encountering numerous life-altering moments, but one incident stands out as the most embarrassing experience in my medical career. It occurred a couple of years ago, when I was consulted by a retired English professor in her 70s, who was diagnosed with pancreatic cancer. During our initial consultation, she presented with symptoms such as pain, nausea, and vomiting, which were causing her immense distress. As a physician, I had the opportunity to discuss these symptoms with her and explore potential treatment options. She specifically asked me about the potential benefits of medical marijuana, and I must admit that my knowledge on the subject was limited, as I had received minimal training on the topic during my medical education.

Chris anderson: hello, ted community, welcome back for another live conversation. It's a big one today, as big as they get. You know, when we created this "build back better" series our thought was how could we address issues arising out of the pandemic, how could we imagine building back from that. But the events of this past week, the horrific death of george floyd and the daily protests that have followed, i mean, they provided a new urgency which we, of course, simply have to address. I mean, can we build back better from this? i think before we can even start to answer that question, we just have to seek to understand the immensity of this moment.whitney pennington rodgers: that's right, chris.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is situated in the heart of the largest and most intact forest in the world, canada's boreal forest. It stretches right across northern canada, in labrador, it's home to the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world: the george river caribou herd, numbering approximately 400,000 animals. Unfortunately, when i was there, i couldn't find one of them, but you have the antlers as proof.all across the boreal, we're blessed with this incredible abundance of wetlands. Wetlands, globally, are one of the most endangered ecosystems. They're absolutely critical ecosystems, they clean air, they clean water, they sequester large amounts of greenhouse gases, and they're home to a huge diversity of species.

Even nature's most disgusting creatures have important secrets, but who would want a swarm of cockroaches coming towards them?yet one of the greatest differences between natural and human technologies relates to robustness. Robust systems are stable in complex and new environments. Remarkably, cockroaches can self-stabilize running over rough terrain. When we put a jet pack on them, or give them a perturbation like an earthquake, we discovered that their wonderfully tuned legs allow them to self-stabilize without using any of their brainpower. They can go over complex terrain like grass, no problem, and not get destabilized. We discovered a new behavior where, because of their shape, they actually roll automatically to their side to go through this artificial test bit of grass.robust systems can perform multiple tasks with the same structure. Here's a new behavior we've discovered.

On October 4, 1957, the world held its breath as the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik, the first-ever artificial satellite. This small metal sphere, under two feet in diameter, sparked a space race between the United States and the USSR that spanned eighteen years and profoundly altered the course of history. It's worth noting, however, that Sputnik was not the first human-made object in space. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, which was used by Germany to target Allied cities in the waning days of World War II—a tactic that, while not particularly effective, laid the groundwork for future space endeavors. At the war's end, both the United States and the Soviet Union seized this technology and the brilliant minds behind it, utilizing them to advance their own aerospace ambitions.

There is growing discontent within the Cabinet over plans to divert funds from Whitehall to ease the pressure on council tax bills, as acknowledged by Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown announced the reallocation of £512 million from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford mentioned that he has engaged in "pretty frank discussions" with his fellow ministers regarding these plans. He emphasized the importance of local governments providing quality services without imposing significant increases in council tax. The reallocated funds are part of a £1 billion initiative designed to help local authorities in England maintain council tax increases below 5% in the upcoming year, coinciding with what is anticipated to be a general election year. Mr. Raynsford also noted that nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in ensuring local authorities are well managed.

The interfacial spin-orbit coupling in Josephson junctions presents a fascinating platform for merging anomalous Hall and Josephson phenomena within a unified device architecture. Through theoretical investigation, we examine how the interplay of these effects influences the transport characteristics of superconductor/ferromagnetic insulator/superconductor heterostructures. Our analysis reveals that the spin-dependent skew tunneling of Cooper pairs across the magnetically active interface generates substantial transverse Hall supercurrents—a phenomenon we term the anomalous Josephson Hall effect.  

By extending the established Furusaki-Tsukada formalism, originally developed for conventional Josephson tunneling currents, we derive a generalized framework to quantify these transverse current components. Our results show that their magnitudes can be precisely tuned by modulating either the spin-orbit coupling strength or the superconducting phase gradient across the junction. Furthermore, we identify sharply localized subgap bound states near the interface, serving as a definitive spectroscopic signature of these Josephson junctions.

Novel concepts, perspectives and challenges in measuring and controlling an open quantum system via sequential schemes are shown. We discuss how similar protocols, relying both on repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses, can allow to: (i) confine and protect quantum dynamics from decoherence in accordance with the zeno physics. (ii) analytically predict the probability that a quantum system is transferred into a target quantum state by means of stochastic sequential measurements. (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise sources by orthogonalizing in the frequency domain the filter functions driving the designed quantum-sensor. The achievement of these tasks will enhance our capability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, thus bringing advances to quantum science and technologies.

It's been a whirlwind few days around here, but I've managed to carve out some time for stress-relieving activities, which I've found to be an absolute necessity. Unfortunately, I must admit that I'm not exactly a master of 8-ball and ping pong. In fact, my friend Rick, affectionately known as the "Canuck," consistently leaves me in his dust. We have a ping pong table in the lobby of our building, which has become a popular spot for a quick game during breaks. Last night, I participated in an 8-ball tournament at our recreation tent, which is equipped with a popcorn machine, multiple TVs, a jukebox, DVD rentals, a pool table, dartboard, and of course, the ever-present ping pong table. I had a fantastic time, despite my less-than-stellar performance - I even managed to scratch on the eight ball. However, the highlight of the evening was the two Dutch soldiers who joined in the tournament, bringing an infectious energy to the event.

"The Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe (OSCE) was invited by the U.S. State Department to observe the upcoming presidential election. The monitoring team, drawn from the OSCE’s Office for Democratic Institutions and Human Rights, marks the first time such international observers will be present for a U.S. election.  

Meanwhile, a group of thirteen congressional Democrats—including four representatives from New York—has formally requested that U.N. Secretary-General Kofi Annan deploy international monitors to Florida to oversee the voting process. However, I respectfully disagree with this proposal. Available information suggests that the OSCE’s role is to study the U.S. electoral system rather than to supervise it—a distinction that renders the earlier call for U.N. intervention both politically motivated and unnecessary.

The vibrant dyes used in freeze pops should come with a disclaimer: "May induce delightful absurdity into your life." While laughter is welcome, one can't help but question whether we'd have ended up perched on a fire escape, braving gusty winds with beers in hand, had we not consumed those technicolor treats earlier. Our adventure led us to the shuttered entrance of a closed bar, where we paused to leaf through a newly delivered office supplies catalog—a fortunate decision indeed. Who could have imagined such mundane pages concealed once-in-a-lifetime deals? Certainly not me. Among the treasures: a free stunt kite with 36 permanent markers, or a complimentary barbecue with 30 lever arch files. The universe works in mysterious ways.

The notion of criminalizing flag desecration struck me as intriguing—perhaps even a potential defense against accusations of witchcraft. As an international blogger, I’ve generally hesitated to engage in certain cultural debates, especially those involving national symbols. However, having initiated this discourse (the conversation itself, not the act of burning flags), I feel compelled to share my perspective.  

Fundamentally, legislating against flag-burning is absurd. When we grant any symbol—regardless of its significance—such unquestioning veneration and legal safeguarding, we inch dangerously closer to authoritarianism. Symbols are inherently mutable; their power lies not in their intrinsic value but in the meanings we ascribe to them or impose upon them.

Undertale comes together to make something greater than the sum of its parts. The graphics are solid for a retro title and the music is nice, but paired together they make some truly beautiful and impacting moments. The script runs the gammut from comedic to dramatic to heartbreaking to hopeful, sometimes all at once, and does this while turning rpg tropes on their head. And all of this without touching on the world, characters, story, gameplay... I could write whole pages about it. But instead, i'll just say this: if you like rpgs, play this game. If you like video games, play this game. If you have seven free hours between now and your death, play this game.

Metal ion implantation into ceramics has been demonstrated to be an effective and controllable technique for tailoring the surface electrical conductivity of the ceramic piece, and this approach has been used in a number of applications. Importantly, it provides a method for grading the voltage drop across high voltage insulators, and thereby increasing the maximum operational voltage that can be applied across the insulator without surface flashover. However a concern for the use of the method is the long term stability of the implantation-induced conductivity, this especially so if the implanted metal species is readily oxidized. Here we report on our examination of the long-term behavior of the surface conductivity of titanium-implanted alumina. The results indicate that after an initial drop of as much as 40% in the first few weeks after implantation, the conductivity shows only a very slow decrease of about 10% over the following year.

Elucidating the emergence of unconventional superconductivity with elevated critical temperatures in strongly correlated two-dimensional systems remains a pivotal challenge in condensed matter physics. Recently, ultracold two-dimensional Fermi gases have gained prominence as pristine and manipulable model systems, enabling the investigation of the intricate interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. However, empirical evidence of superfluidity in these systems has long been elusive. In this study, we provide definitive proof of superfluidity in an ultracold two-dimensional Fermi gas by introducing a periodic potential and observing the absence of dissipation at velocities below a critical threshold, v$_{\rm c}$. Furthermore, our measurements of v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength reveal a pronounced maximum in the crossover regime, where bosonic and fermionic superfluidity converge. This breakthrough establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a versatile and powerful platform for exploring the profound impact of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

Headlines can be misleading, as a single study can be cherry-picked to support a sensational claim. For instance, a single vitamin may reduce the risk of heart disease, while eating chocolate can alleviate stress in students. However, a new drug may prolong the lives of patients with a rare disease. The disconnect between broad, attention-grabbing headlines and the incremental results of medical research can be significant. It's essential to separate the wheat from the chaff by examining the original research behind the headlines. To achieve this, we've developed a hypothetical research scenario for each of these three headlines. By analyzing the explanation provided in each scenario, you can assess the credibility of the headline and avoid being misled. These scenarios are designed to simplify the process of evaluating the credibility of a headline, making it easier for you to make an informed decision.

Upon completing my dual degree in molecular and cell biology and linguistics from the University of California, Berkeley, seven years prior, I attended a campus career fair that led to an interview with a startup called Theranos. At the time, information about the company was scarce, but what was available was undeniably impressive. Theranos was pioneering a revolutionary medical device that enabled comprehensive blood panel analysis from a mere finger-stick sample, eliminating the need for invasive venipuncture. This innovation not only promised a more comfortable experience for patients but also held the potential to unlock the doors to predictive diagnostics, a groundbreaking prospect that sparked my interest.

October 4, 1957, marked a pivotal moment in history as the Soviet Union successfully launched Sputnik, the world's inaugural artificial satellite, into the vast expanse of space. This diminutive, metallic orb, measuring less than two feet in diameter, ignited a fierce space race between the United States and the Soviet Union, a rivalry that would span nearly two decades and irrevocably alter the global landscape. Although Sputnik was the first satellite to be launched into space, it was not the first human-made object to breach the Earth's atmosphere. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, employed by Germany in a desperate bid to launch missile attacks against Allied cities during the waning years of World War II. Despite its limited effectiveness, the V-2 rocket's technology and the scientists behind its development were seized by both the United States and the Soviet Union, who subsequently repurposed them to fuel their own ambitious projects, laying the groundwork for the impending space age.

The concept of ahimsa, or noninjury, is a fundamental principle to living beings. As Mahatma Gandhi's concept of ahimsa evolved over time, I became more mindful of my thoughts and words. The lines in the anthem 'scatter her enemies and make them fall; confound their policies, frustrate their knavish tricks particularly jarred upon my sentiments of ahimsa.' Mahatma Gandhi's story of his experiments with truth, as recorded in his book 'My Experiments with Truth' in 1927, is a testament to the enduring relevance of his life and message to our lives today. Today, October 2, marks the birthday of Mahatma Gandhi, who was born in 1869. A man born over two centuries ago may seem irrelevant to our era, but his life and teachings are still highly applicable to our lives today.

We introduce a novel mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator for planetary systems that seamlessly resolves close encounters. Our approach leverages a time regularization technique to maintain the stability properties of symplectic integrators while significantly reducing the effective step size during close encounters. By employing a high-order MVS scheme, we are able to integrate systems with large time steps far away from close encounters, ensuring accurate long-term behavior. We demonstrate the effectiveness of our algorithm by applying it to a system of six super-Earths undergoing strong scattering over a period of 50,000 years. Our results show that our method outperforms hybrid methods such as Mercury in terms of energy conservation, while providing the same level of accuracy.

Cardinal criticises iraq war cost billions of pounds spent on conflict in iraq and in the middle east should have been used to reduce poverty, cardinal cormac murphy-o'connor has said. The head of the catholic church in england and wales made the comments on bbc radio 4 and will re-iterate his stance in his christmas midnight mass. The cardinal used a christmas message to denounce the war in iraq as a "terrible" waste of money. He and the archbishop of canterbury have both spoken out about the war. Speaking on bbc radio 4's thought for the day slot, he criticised the fact that "billions" have been spent on war, instead of being used to bring people "out of dire poverty and malnourishment and disease". The cardinal said 2005 should be the year for campaigning to "make history poverty".

Holla so i saw troy tonight with my family...i was not impressed. I'm too tired to go way into specifics, but i do think it had potential. However, i think too much energy was spent on the set (which was magnificent) and not enough on creating a solidifying tone, theme, etc. It retold the story, but it was not particularly gripping, and i don't feel like i gained anything from watching it. The character development was rather weak. Additionally, i thought the score was poor, and was basically a knock-off of the gladiator. The gladiator used the background music of a moaning woman and it worked b/c it backed up and complemented a genuinely gripping narrative.

A Georgia woman has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that the company's printer ink cartridges are designed with a secret expiration date, resulting in unnecessary replacements. According to the complaint, a microchip embedded in the cartridge deceives the printer into believing it requires refilling, even when a substantial amount of ink remains. The lawsuit aims to represent a class of individuals who have purchased HP inkjet printers in the United States since February 2001. HP, the world's leading printer manufacturer, has declined to comment on the matter. While HP's ink cartridges utilize chip technology to detect low ink levels and prompt users to replace them, the lawsuit claims that this technology also triggers an automatic shutdown of the cartridges on a predetermined date, regardless of the actual ink levels.

For those in their thirties, especially in the UK, this is a delightful trip down memory lane. It hearkens back to the days of enduring the often-aggravating Timmy Mallett just to catch the five-minute Transformers cartoon segment. Familiar voices like Optimus Prime, Megatron, and Bumblebee make their return, instantly sparking recognition. The iconic transformation sound from the cartoons is faithfully recreated. Visually, the cel-shaded graphics are spot on, perfectly capturing the essence of Generation One. Beyond nostalgia, this is an incredibly enjoyable game with expertly crafted combat mechanics. Transforming during battles is frequently necessary and seamlessly integrated into combos, offering a unique experience compared to High Moon's outstanding War for Cybertron and Fall of Cybertron games. Additionally, there is a significant emphasis on melee combat, although certain foes, such as the Seekers, necessitate switching to ranged blasters for effective engagement.

In its early development stages, I was an eager supporter of this game, purchasing it when the sole currency was Spacebux and the tech tree was nonexistent. It was during this period that the game truly shone. The arsenal of guns was remarkably balanced, with each firearm being equally formidable, thus eliminating any concerns about imbalance. As a result, every player had an equal opportunity to engage and defeat others at any given time. Furthermore, the game generously provided unlimited gun slots and five customizable loadouts, allowing for unparalleled flexibility and creativity. Given the game's boastful claim of featuring an astronomical number of guns, it is perplexing that the current iteration only offers a meager six weapon slots and three loadouts, with additional slots and loadouts available only through in-game purchases. In my heyday, I had amassed an impressive collection of guns, spanning multiple pages, and was constantly brainstorming innovative ideas for new firearms, as this aspect of the game was where I derived the most enjoyment.

This study presents a novel extension of existing human gait models, drawing inspiration from the works of Keller and Kockshenev. By integrating a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a non-linear description of the mass center's oscillatory motion, the hybrid model aims to accurately replicate the walk-run transition phenomenon. The analysis of experimental data across various speed regimes reveals that the trajectory perimeter of the mass center is a crucial parameter in quantifying this dynamic behavior. This research was conducted in collaboration with the biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratory research groups at the Manuela Beltrán University in Bogotá, Colombia, utilizing a specially designed experimental protocol.

This game was a surprising gem. One often sees rave reviews of certain products that are often misleading. In this case, the reviews reflect the fun of this neat little game. It is an odd mix between a classic 2d platformer and an moba, and does it with style. I found myself enjoying characters that i wouldn't enjoy in other moba games. I had expected this trait to continue, but decided to give a melee tank a go anyway. I was pleasantly surprised with the outcome and ended up trying out more as they were unlocked from playing games. The art style and interface is oddly charming. As such, i now use one of the characters as my avatar and a wallpaper from creating a card set for the game.

Jason Robinson, the captain of England's rugby union team, is aiming for a significant dual-code victory against Australia this Saturday. Having transitioned from rugby league to rugby union in 2000, Robinson will lead England as they face Australia at Twickenham at 14:30 GMT. Later, at 18:15 GMT, Great Britain's rugby league team will meet Australia in the Tri-Nations tournament final. Robinson stated, "Defeating the Aussies in both matches would be a tremendous accomplishment, particularly for rugby league." Simultaneously, Great Britain has the opportunity to break a 34-year win drought against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

The aftermath of returning from practicum has been challenging. During the first week, I struggled significantly, but it's in this second week that things have started to become clearer. The post-practicum distress seems to be a widespread issue that is just now gaining attention. I was deeply affected, but a meeting with the course coordinator provided relief. This meeting allowed me to voice my concerns and, in doing so, ensure that no one else would be placed in a similarly inadequate practicum environment. Yesterday, I encountered a distressed classmate who was adamant about warning another student scheduled for her previous school about its poor quality. I managed to calm her down, suggesting that it would be more effective to approach the administrator first.

Sid Meier's Civilization  Beyond Earth is a turn-based game that challenges you to build your civilization to achieve a specific goal or 'victory' on a new planet. Unlike previous games in the Civilization series, this installment takes a bold approach by setting the game in the future, allowing players to colonize a new planet and navigate its unique challenges. One of the most innovative features of this game is the introduction of the affinity system and orbital layer, which adds a new layer of depth to the gameplay experience. However, some players may find the game overwhelming at times, particularly with the sheer amount of new mechanics introduced. To further enhance the game, it would be beneficial to incorporate more diverse and visually stunning artwork, such as the environment, to create a more immersive experience when interacting with other leaders. This could include the ability to explore and experience the environment in a more engaging and visually appealing way.

Magic fans beware... Wizards is afraid to make a decent digital game. At its core magic the gathering is a social game. A game to enjoy with your friends (or frenemies) laughing, talking smack, and dropping game winning bombs. Wizards of the coast seems to have forgotten this. If you choose to play with friends you cant earn gold, and gold is important. Gold is so important that they have implemented a daily cap, understandable considering gold unlocks everything in game. But get ready for a long and boring 1v1 grind against the computer or random oponents, whom you can't even speak to. If you choose to play for free, it will take you roughly 4 hours a day for upwards of a month, by yourself, to unlock the cards.

If I can overlook the minor bugs, which I personally experienced only two  one minor and the other quite frustrating, I can confidently recommend this game for space exploration enthusiasts. The recent edit has significantly improved my frame rate, increasing it from 60-90 to 80-120 frames per second. With its impressive specifications, including an i5-6600k processor, Radeon RX480 graphics card, 16GB DDR4 RAM, and a budget-friendly price tag of around 650€, this gaming rig is an excellent option for those seeking a high-performance gaming experience. Although some users may argue that this setup is overkill, I can attest that I've experienced a consistent 60+ frames per second on high settings with vsync enabled, which is significantly higher than the 20 frames per second reported by many users. Overall, I highly recommend this game for those seeking an immersive space exploration experience.

When I gaze into the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. (cheering) I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison—six years, to be exact—starting as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost a man his life. But what got me from there to here wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in adult prison or the harshness of our legal system. Instead, it was a learning environment of a classroom that introduced me to something I didn't think was possible for me or our justice system as a whole. A few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered in the prison.

Podcasting is the creation and distribution of audio content over the internet, typically through a series of episodes that can be downloaded or streamed by listeners. The term "podcasting" was coined to describe the practice of creating and distributing audio content using an Apple iPod, but it has since become a broader term to refer to any type of digital audio content.
Podcasters create and produce their shows in a variety of formats, including interviews, news, music, and storytelling, and they can use a variety of platforms and tools to distribute their content.

Adam Curry is a well-known proponent of podcasting and has been credited with coining the term "podcasting." He was a host and VJ (video jockey) on MTV, and he began creating and distributing his own podcasts in the early 2000s. Curry's podcasts covered a wide range of topics, including music, politics, and culture, and they quickly gained popularity due to their unique blend of humor and insight.

Podcasting has become a popular medium for creators to share their ideas and perspectives with a global audience. It allows them to produce high-quality audio content without the need for expensive studios or equipment, and it provides a level of flexibility and creative control that is not possible with traditional radio or television.

I had no idea; I thought it wasn't the most eloquent argument, but I suppose someone thought otherwise.

Sir Alex Ferguson has lavished praise on Manchester United's gutsy performance in their 1-0 win over Aston Villa, describing it as their most challenging away game of the season. 'That was a fantastic game of football, end-to-end with lots of good passing,' he said. 'We showed a lot of character and guts, and we weren't going to lose. I look at that fixture and think we've been there and won, while Arsenal and Chelsea have yet to come, and Villa may have some players back when they do.' Ferguson also praised senior stars Ryan Giggs and Roy Keane, who came off the bench for the injured John O'Shea. 'Roy came on and brought a bit of composure to the midfield, which we needed, and which no other player has got.'

The kindness of strangers never ceases to amaze me. Today's laundry adventure with my children in tow became a vivid reminder of how mundane tasks can swiftly transform into chaotic challenges. Yet amidst the chaos, I discovered something beautiful—the unexpected warmth of strangers who offered help simply because they wanted to. Let me be clear: this wasn't some ulterior-motivated gesture. Between the toddler drool stains and my disheveled laundry-day attire, I was far from alluring. Frankly, with my current hygiene standards, there wasn't even a faint scent-based incentive!  

At this moment, I'm multitasking at my desk—crafting this post while nostalgically watching No Doubt's greatest hits. The perfect soundtrack for reflecting on life's small but meaningful moments.

Downloads enter us singles chart digital music downloads are being included in the main us singles chart for the first time. Billboard's hot 100 chart now incorporates data from sales of music downloads, previously only assigned to a separate download chart. Green day's boulevard of broken dreams is currently number two in billboard's pop chart, and tops its digital chart. Download sales are due to be incorporated into the uk singles chart later this year. Digital sales in the us are already used to compile billboard's hot digital sales chart. They will now be tallied with sales of physical singles and airplay information to make up its new hot 100 chart. Its second new chart - the pop 100 - also combines airplay, digital and physical sales but confines its airplay information to us radio stations which play chart music.

Davies favours gloucester future wales hooker mefin davies is likely to stay with english side gloucester despite reported interest from the neath-swansea ospreys. Bbc wales understands the ospreys are interested in the 32-year-old, but that he would prefer to stay where he is. Andrea Levy's novel, 'Small Island', has won the Whitbread Novel of the Year award, marking a major achievement for the author. Levy's book, which was previously a favourite to win the overall prize, has beaten off the Booker Prize-winning author, Alan Hollinghurst, for his novel 'The Line of Beauty'. Meanwhile, Geraldine McCaughrean has taken home the children's fiction award for 'Not the End of the World' for the third time. The Whitbread Book of the Year award is a prestigious honour, with a £25,000 prize attached to it. A panel of judges, comprising Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant, and writer Joanne Harris, will meet on January 25 to decide the overall winner, with the announcement being made later that evening. Bookmaker William Hill has placed London-based Levy's novel as the 6/4 favourite to win, reflecting the strong public backing for her work.

- The game is marred by numerous bugs, particularly in the environment, which can cause frustration and even lead to becoming stuck or having your shots hit an invisible wall, despite having a clear line of sight on the enemy.
- The game's hand-holding and babysitting approach can be overly reliant on the AI, making it feel more like a chore to play.
- Enemies seem to have a God-like accuracy, making it disconcerting when you're spotted, like the first Ghost Warrior.
- The game's checkpoints and saves are also subpar, adding to the overall frustration.

Even then, playing this game feels like an extremely tedious task, only done to check it off my library and move on to the next game. Considering the time I wasted on the first Ghost Warrior (which was equally underwhelming), I'm likely to put off playing this one until the very end.

The revival of the Devil May Cry franchise has ushered in a transformative era for the series, with its iconic protagonist, Dante, undergoing a significant metamorphosis. Although die-hard fans of the original series may initially lament the changes to their cherished character, this bold new direction, as exemplified by Ninja Theory's latest installment, is undoubtedly a step in the right direction. DmC serves as an ideal entry point for newcomers to the series, offering a captivating origin story that delves into the complexities of a once-arrogant and cynical Dante, and chronicles his poignant journey towards self-discovery and altruism. Through a masterfully woven narrative arc, the game breathes new life into the character, rendering him a compelling and relatable figure to behold. As the story unfolds, Dante is thrust into a corrupt world he had previously chosen to ignore, marking the beginning of a transformative odyssey that will challenge his perceptions and test his resolve.

We study the transport properties and superconducting proximity effect in nsn junctions formed by a time-reversal symmetry broken weyl semimetal (wsm) in proximity to an $s$-wave superconductor. We find that the differential conductance and induced pairing amplitudes strongly depend on the angle between the junction direction in real space and the axis separating the weyl nodes in momentum space. We identify the influence of a chiral chemical potential, i.e., the electron population imbalance between weyl nodes of opposite chirality, on the transport characteristics of the junction. Remarkably, we observe a net spin polarization of cooper pairs that are generated via andreev reflection in the two wsm regions. The spin polarization is opposite in the two wsm regions and highly sensitive to the chirality imbalance and excitation energy.

A recent citizenship ceremony in Jacksonville has sparked controversy, prompting the relocation of future naturalization events, after allegations surfaced that Republican activists exploited the occasion to register new citizens as GOP members. The June 29 ceremony, attended by nearly 200 people, appeared to proceed without incident, culminating in the attendees being sworn in as new American citizens. However, a peculiar incident unfolded as the newly naturalized citizens prepared to depart. An immigration official, overseeing the swearing-in process, encouraged the crowd to visit a nearby voter registration table, a common feature at such events. Nevertheless, this particular table was staffed by Republican volunteers, who had pre-selected the party affiliation box on the registration forms, effectively enrolling the new citizens as Republicans. This unusual practice raised suspicions among some attendees, including Linda Cross, who was present to witness her husband, Dario Cruz, take the citizenship oath.

On October 4, 1957, humanity witnessed a pivotal moment in history when the Soviet Union propelled Sputnik—the first artificial satellite—into Earth's orbit. This unassuming sphere, barely 60 centimeters wide, ignited an intense Cold War rivalry between the United States and the USSR, sparking an eighteen-year space race that would fundamentally reshape global politics, science, and culture. While Sputnik marked the dawn of the space age, it wasn't humanity's initial technological incursion beyond our atmosphere. That distinction belongs to Germany's V-2 rockets, which were deployed as ballistic missiles against Allied targets during World War II's desperate final stages. Though militarily ineffective, these rockets represented a technological breakthrough that both superpowers eagerly appropriated—along with their German creators—laying the foundation for their subsequent space programs and the new era of exploration they would pioneer.

Renowned scholar Arun Shourie draws attention to the prescient warning issued by the current Governor of Uttar Pradesh, dating back to 1996, regarding the looming threat of a 'Third Islamic State' emerging in the subcontinent. T.V. Rajeswar, the former Director of the Intelligence Bureau and current Governor of Uttar Pradesh, had previously sounded the alarm in a series of articles published in the Hindustan Times. Shourie poses a pertinent question  does the revelation that Rajeswar, rather than himself or Mr. Advani, was the author of these warnings lend credibility to the predictions, or does it instead lead one to conclude that Rajeswar is a closet communalist, thereby disqualifying him from continuing as Governor of Uttar Pradesh?

Deep within the vast expanse of Canada's boreal forest, the world's largest and most pristine wilderness, lies a colossal environmental and industrial endeavor. Spanning the breadth of northern Canada, this region is home to the Labrador wilderness, where the majestic George River caribou herd roams free. As one of the last remaining wild caribou herds, it boasts an impressive population of approximately 400,000 animals. Although a personal encounter with these magnificent creatures proved elusive, the presence of their antlers serves as a poignant reminder of their existence. The boreal forest is also blessed with an astonishing array of wetlands, ecosystems that are not only globally endangered but also crucial for maintaining ecological balance. These wetlands play a vital role in purifying the air and water, sequestering substantial amounts of greenhouse gases, and providing a habitat for a diverse array of species, underscoring their importance in the grand tapestry of our planet's ecosystem.

The Cabinet is expressing anger at the proposal to use Whitehall's cash to keep council tax bills down, with local government minister Nick Raynsford acknowledging the plans. In his pre-budget report on Thursday, Gordon Brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets. Mr. Raynsford stated that he had engaged in "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers over the plans. He emphasized that local governments must deliver good services without significant council tax increases. The central government's cash is part of a £1bn package to assist local authorities in England in keeping next year's council tax rises below 5%, likely in the context of a general election. Mr. Raynsford noted that nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in well-run local authorities.

When random effects are correlated with sample design variables, the traditional approach of employing individual survey weights (constructed to be inversely proportional to the unit survey inclusion probabilities) to form a pseudo-likelihood no longer produces asymptotically unbiased inference. We construct a weight-exponentiated formulation for the random effects distribution that achieves unbiased inference for generating hyperparameters of the random effects. We contrast our approach with frequentist methods that rely on numerical integration to reveal that only the Bayesian method achieves both unbiased estimation with respect to the sampling design distribution and consistency with respect to the population generating distribution. Our simulations and real data example for a survey of business establishments demonstrate the utility of our approach across different modeling formulations and sampling designs. This work serves as a capstone for recent developmental efforts that combine traditional survey estimation approaches with the Bayesian modeling paradigm and provides a bridge across the two rich but disparate sub-fields.

Anti-spam laws bite spammer hard; net's self-declared spam king seeks bankruptcy protection.

Scott Richter, the man behind OptinRealBig.com and billions of junk mail messages, said lawsuits had forced the company into chapter 11. OptinRealBig was fighting several legal battles, most notably against Microsoft, which is pushing for millions of dollars in damages. The company said filing for chapter 11 would help it try to resolve its legal problems but still keep trading.

Listed as the third biggest spammer in the world by Junk Mail Watchdog SpamHaus, OptinRealBig was sued in December 2003 for sending mail messages that violated anti-spam laws. The lawsuit was brought by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer, who alleged that Mr. Richter and his accomplices sent billions of spam messages through 514 compromised Net addresses in 35 countries.

England's dual code rugby union captain, Jason Robinson, has set his sights on securing a dual code success against Australia on Saturday. The former rugby league international, who switched codes in 2000, will lead England against Australia at Twickenham at 2 30 GMT. The 14 15 GMT kick-off marks a crucial match for England, who have the opportunity to secure their third consecutive international victory after successive wins over Canada and South Africa, as well as exacting revenge for their 51-15 thrashing by Australia in June. A win would be a massive achievement, especially for league. England have the chance to seal their third autumn international victory after successive wins over Canada and South Africa, and put an end to 34 years of disappointment against Australia with a victory at Elland Road.

We developed single-shot shaped pulses for ultra high fidelity (uh-fidelity) population transfer on a 3-level quantum system in lambda configuration. To ensure high fidelity, we use the lewis-riesenfeld (l-r) method to derive a family of solutions leading to an exact transfer, where the solutions follow a single dynamical mode of the l-r invariant. Among this family, we identify a tracking solution with a single parameter to control simultaneously the fidelity of the transfer, the population of the excited state, and robustness. We define a measure of the robustness of an uh-fidelity transfer as the minimum percentile deviation on the pulse areas at which the infidelity rises above $10^{-4}$. The robustness of our shaped pulses is found superior to that of gaussian and adiabatically-optimized pulses for moderate pulse areas.

Investigating the interplay between a time-reversal symmetry broken Weyl semimetal and an s-wave superconductor, we uncover the intricate transport properties and superconducting proximity effect in nsn junctions. Notably, the differential conductance and induced pairing amplitudes exhibit a pronounced dependence on the angular alignment between the junction's spatial orientation and the momentum-space axis separating the Weyl nodes. Furthermore, our analysis reveals the significant impact of a chiral chemical potential, arising from the electron population imbalance between Weyl nodes of opposing chirality, on the junction's transport characteristics. A striking observation is the emergence of a net spin polarization in Cooper pairs generated through Andreev reflection in the two Weyl semimetal regions, with the polarization being oppositely directed in each region and exhibiting high sensitivity to both the chirality imbalance and excitation energy.

This game is an absolute masterpiece of strategy! Having played every other war game (European Escalation and Albion), I must say that this one stands out. The sheer number of units is impressive. While I'm not entirely sure how many units there are, it's safe to say there are a substantial amount. The graphics are stunning, the sound design is excellent, and the overall gameplay is nothing short of epic. The in-game chat box at the main menu is also an added bonus, and I'm not being facetious when I say it's quite entertaining. However, if you're new to the strategy genre or underestimate the complexity of this game, it may take some time to get accustomed to it. Don't be discouraged if you lose units – it's all part of the learning process. Just make sure to plan ahead, always maintain a strong defense, and adapt to the situation at hand.

Although not without its flaws, this game has provided a highly enjoyable experience. The save system and the potential for sudden character death can be frustrating. During my first playthrough, I lost my main character around the 10-hour mark of a 20-hour game. I would have appreciated having a save point to restart from at that moment. To address this, I've started using a third-party mod or save tool to prevent such setbacks. Additionally, the aiming reticle feels a bit too small, and the zoom feature could be more powerful. Despite these issues, the game has been a thrilling ride. While I don't usually gravitate towards zombie-themed games, the blend of strategy, missions, and the first-person shooter/beat 'em up elements is fantastic. I find myself playing whenever I can, always tempted to complete just one more mission or make one more supply run—or sometimes escaping to a safe spot before I log out.

In the context of algebraic topology, a global Mackey functor is a functor that assigns to a topological space a ring of coefficients that can be used to define various topological invariants, such as homotopy groups and homology groups.

 The global Thom spectra $\mathbfmo$ and \mathbfmu$ are certain spectra of sheaves of sets that are associated to the topological space $X$ and the smooth structure on $X$. The splittings of the Thom spectra imply that certain long exact homotopy groups separate into short exact sequences of the form $1 \to \pi_i(X) \to \pi_i(X,A) \to \pi_i(Y,A) \to 1$, where $A$ is an abelian group and $Y$ is a topological space.

The long exact sequences of homotopy groups that arise from the splittings of the Thom spectra can be used to define various topological invariants of $X$, such as the Euler class, the Betti numbers, and the Hurewicz group. These invariants are related to the tautological representations of $o(n)$ and $u(n)$, which are functions on $X$ that encode information about the homotopy type of $X$.

The comedy "Sideways" triumphed at this year’s Independent Spirit Awards, sweeping all six categories in which it was nominated. It earned the title of Best Film, while Alexander Payne received accolades for Best Director and Best Screenplay, sharing the writing honor with his collaborator, Jim Taylor. The film's stars, Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen, also took home acting awards. "Sideways" is expected to perform well at Sunday’s Oscars, having secured five nominations. Celebrating its 20th anniversary, the Independent Spirit Awards recognize films produced outside the conventional studio system and traditionally take place the day preceding the Oscars. Other notable winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who clinched the Best Actress award for her portrayal of a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama "Maria Full of Grace." Moreno is also an Oscar nominee for Best Actress. Additionally, the Best First Screenplay award was presented to Joshua Marston for "Maria Full of Grace.

On october 4, 1957, the world watched in awe and fear as the soviet union launched sputnik, the world's first man-made satellite, into space. This little metal ball, smaller than two feet in diameter, launched a space race between the u.s. And u.s.s.r. That would last for eighteen years and change the world as we know it. Sputnik was actually not the first piece of human technology to enter space. That superlative goes to the v-2 rocket used by germany in missile attacks against allied cities as a last-ditch effort in the final years of world war ii. It wasn't very effective, but, at the end of the war, both the u.s. And u.s.s.r. Had captured the technology and the scientists that had developed it and began using them for their own projects.

Low-symmetry 2d materials---such as res$_2$ and rese$_2$ monolayers, black phosphorus monolayers, group-iv monochalcogenide monolayers, borophene, among others---have more complex atomistic structures than the honeycomb lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials give rise to inhomogeneous electron, optical, valley, and spin responses, as well as entirely new properties such as ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations can also take place due to low symmetry. The "beyond graphene: low-symmetry and anisotropic 2d materials" special topic was assembled to highlight recent experimental and theoretical research on these emerging materials.

Our institution has become the final receptacle for society's most systemic failures. I cannot specify who seeks our services or the duration of their stay—we inherit those for whom every other intervention has proven inadequate, individuals who have slipped through every layer of our social welfare infrastructure. When all other systems fail to support them, the responsibility falls to us: to manage, to restrain. Through decades of practice, both as a correctional system and as a nation, we've perfected this function—yet this proficiency should bring no pride. Currently, our nation incarcerates a greater proportion of its population than any other country globally. The number of African American men currently in our custody surpasses the total enslaved in 1850.

Arnesen denies rift with santini tottenham sporting director frank arnesen has denied that coach jacques santini resigned because of a clash of personalities at white hart lane. There had been newspaper speculation that santini had felt undermined by arnesen's role at the club. "it is absolutely not true," arnesen told bbc radio five live. "there is only one thing that made him resign and that is his own personal problems. "he has talked to me recently and said this matter is absolutely for himself." arnesen said he was unable to throw any light onto the problems that caused santini to quit after just 13 games in charge. He added: "jacques has never gone into exactly what it was. But i trust him in that; you have to accept it. I think we should respect it.

In this work, a generalization of the study of the human gait was made from already existent models in the literature, like models of keller and kockshenev. In this hybrid model, a strategy of metabolic energy minimization is combined in a race process, with a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration, trying to reproduce the behavior of the walk-run transition. The results of the experimental data, for different speed regimes, indicate that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a relevant quantity in the quantification of this dynamic. An experimental procedure was put into practice in collaboration with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences and laboratories of the manuela beltr\'an university in bogot\'a, colombia.

**Britain's Economic Future Takes Center Stage in Election Campaign**  

Chancellor Gordon Brown has declared that the nation's economic prospects will form the cornerstone of Labour's election strategy. His remarks followed the final cabinet meeting at 10 Downing Street ahead of the anticipated election announcement. Brown emphasized that voters would acknowledge Labour's record of delivering economic stability and sustained growth—a commitment the party pledges to uphold.  

Meanwhile, the Conservatives unveiled initiatives to address antisocial behavior, while the Liberal Democrats expanded on their plans to reform local taxation.  

In a notable intervention, the Archbishop of Canterbury issued an open letter to all major political parties, urging them to refrain from exploiting public anxieties during the campaign. He implored leaders to avoid reducing the election to a contest of fearmongering over issues such as terrorism, immigration, and crime.

In a striking juxtaposition, two twin domes embody the dichotomy of design philosophies. One, a marvel of modern engineering, comprises thousands of intricately assembled steel components, while the other, a masterpiece of organic elegance, is crafted from a single, unbroken silk thread. This contrast highlights the fundamental differences between synthetic and organic design, with one imposing its presence on the environment and the other harmoniously integrating with it. The former is a product of human ingenuity, carefully designed to coexist with nature, whereas the latter is an expression of nature's inherent design, shaped by its own intrinsic forces.

This dichotomy is reminiscent of Michelangelo's profound observation that, when gazing upon unshaped marble, he envisioned a figure yearning to be liberated. The renowned artist's chisel was the instrument that freed the captive form, but living organisms defy such rigid manipulation. Instead, they grow and evolve, their cells carrying the genetic blueprint that governs their function, replication, and intricate interconnectedness.

The tools we employ in design have far-reaching consequences, and since the advent of the Industrial Revolution, the design world has been largely dictated by the imperatives of mass production and manufacturing.

Sun Microsystems has revolutionized access to high-performance computing with its innovative pay-per-use grid service. The company now enables businesses to tap into massive computational resources at an unprecedented value—just $1 per hour for processing and storage capabilities hosted on Sun's infrastructure.  

This groundbreaking approach to grid computing represents a paradigm shift in how enterprises view IT resources, positioning computing power as a fundamental utility alongside traditional hardware and software. Sun's leadership draws a compelling parallel between this model and the evolution of electricity distribution, suggesting that on-demand computing could follow a similar trajectory as essential public utilities.  

"Instead of investing in costly private infrastructure, why not leverage our grid for just a dollar an hour?" challenged Jonathan Schwartz, Sun's Chief Operating Officer, during the unveiling at the company's quarterly Network Computing event in California. This bold proposition underscores Sun's vision of making enterprise-grade computing accessible, scalable, and economically efficient for businesses worldwide.

It's 11:14 PM Mountain Standard Time, and I'm seated in my bedroom repairing the personal computer belonging to my workplace's personnel director. While I don't particularly object to helping, I occasionally reflect on how frequently I end up troubleshooting others' computers as a favor. I genuinely appreciate being able to serve and care for people through this skill, but I often question where to draw the line between kindness and being exploited.  

Recently, I've been burning the midnight oil with reading and other activities, so I was eager to turn in early tonight—until practicality prevailed, reminding me it would be wiser to return this computer to its owner sooner rather than later. *Chuckles* It seems there's little respite for those who are perpetually busy. And so, here I am, rewatching *Joe Schmo 2* to pass the time.

Last night, the independent Spirit Awards honored the comedy 'Sideways' with a sweep of six awards, including Best Film. The film's director, Alexander Payne, and co-writer, Jim Taylor, took home the awards for Best Director and Best Screenplay, respectively. The film's stars, Paul Giamatti, Thomas Haden Church, and Virginia Madsen, also received acting awards for their outstanding performances. The Spirit Awards, now in their 20th year, celebrate independent films outside the traditional studio system, and are held the day before the Oscars. Other notable winners included Catalina Sandino Moreno, who won Best Actress for her powerful portrayal of a drug smuggler in the Colombian drama 'Maria Full of Grace.' Moreno is also nominated for Best Actress at the Oscars. The Best First Screenplay award went to Joshua Marston for 'Maria Full of Grace,' a gripping and poignant film that explores the complexities of human nature.

Irish sprinter Paul Brizzel will be one of eight hopefuls from Ireland competing in this weekend's European Indoor Championships in Sheffield. Alongside him, US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll will be the only Irish athletes representing their country in the event. Brizzel, who will be defending his 200m title, is expected to put in a strong performance in the British trials. Meanwhile, James McIlroy is looking to confirm his place in the British team for the Europeans by winning the 800m title. McIlroy has been in top form on the European circuit in recent weeks, and his impressive performances have secured him a spot in the Madrid-bound team. Mark Carroll, who is currently ranked third in the world 3000m ranking, has confirmed that he will join Cragg in the European Championships. The duo's participation in the event is expected to be a highlight of the competition.

The Milky Way's complex assembly history is being gradually revealed, thanks to the wealth of data provided by the Gaia mission. It is probable that the formation of our galaxy's globular cluster system followed a similarly intricate and multifaceted path. To elucidate this process, we investigate the connection between the globular clusters and the known major mergers that have shaped the Milky Way. By combining Gaia's comprehensive kinematic data for nearly all galactic clusters with the largest available sample of cluster ages, carefully corrected for systematic biases, we analyzed the dynamical properties of these clusters, particularly in the context of their integrals of motion. Our findings indicate that approximately 40% of the clusters likely originated within the Milky Way itself, providing valuable insights into the galaxy's formative history.

Green Day's "Boulevard of Broken Dreams" is currently at number two on Billboard's Hot 100 chart, and tops its digital chart. Download sales are expected to be incorporated into the UK singles chart later this year. Digital sales in the US are already used to compile Billboard's Hot Digital Sales chart. They will now be tallied with sales of physical singles and airplay information to make up its new Hot 100 chart. Its second new chart - the Pop 100 - combines airplay, digital, and physical sales, but confines its airplay information to US radio stations that play chart music.

Allan Scott remains optimistic about his medal prospects at the upcoming European Indoor Championships despite a bittersweet debut on the international stage. The 22-year-old Scottish hurdler secured fourth place in the 60m event at Madrid's prestigious José M. Cagigal Memorial Meet, marking his inaugural appearance on the global circuit.  

"While it was undoubtedly a valuable learning experience, I'm still fully committed to contending for a podium finish next week," stated the East Kilbride sprinter.  

The race was dominated by Spain's Felipe Vivancos, who matched the national record, followed by Sweden's Robert Kronberg in second and Haiti's Dudley Dorival in third. Though Scott clinched his heat with a time of 7.64 seconds, a marginal 0.04-second slowdown in the final left him with mixed feelings about his performance. Nevertheless, his strong showing has only reinforced his determination to excel at the championships.

Paul Brizzel to Compete in AAA's Championships in Sheffield

Irish sprinter Paul Brizzel will be among the eight athletes representing Ireland in this weekend's prestigious AAA's Championships in Sheffield. Notably, Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll, who are based in the United States, are the only Irish athletes so far selected for the European Championships who will not compete in Sheffield.

Brizzel aims to defend his 200m title at the British trials. Meanwhile, James McIlroy, currently in exceptional form on the European circuit, is looking to secure his spot in the British team for the Madrid competition by winning the 800m title. With stellar performances this winter, McIlroy is one of the world's fastest 800m runners and is virtually guaranteed a place in Madrid.

Cork's Mark Carroll, ranked third globally in the 3000m, confirmed midweek that he will join Cragg at the European Championships, where Cragg holds the top spot in the 3000m rankings.

One of the most pressing unsolved challenges in condensed matter physics is understanding how two-dimensional systems can exhibit unconventional superconductivity at high critical temperatures. Ultracold Fermi gases have emerged as ideal model systems to investigate the intricate interplay between strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, yet direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been lacking. Here, we provide conclusive evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold Fermi gas by inducing a periodic potential and observing the absence of dissipation below a critical velocity, v_c. Our measurement of v_c as a function of interaction strength reveals a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. This achievement establishes ultracold Fermi gases as a powerful platform for exploring the impact of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids and sheds new light on the fundamental physics governing these systems.

The Cabinet has expressed frustration over Chancellor Gordon Brown's decision to redirect £512 million from central government funds to local budgets, a move revealed in his recent pre-budget report. Local Government Minister Nick Raynsford acknowledged the discontent among ministers, admitting to holding "frank discussions" regarding the proposal.  

Despite the controversy, Raynsford emphasized that local authorities must maintain high-quality services without imposing significant council tax hikes. The reallocated funds form part of a broader £1 billion initiative aimed at capping next year’s council tax increases below 5%—a critical measure ahead of an anticipated general election.  

Raynsford further noted that nearly all central government departments have a vested interest in ensuring efficient local governance, underscoring the broader implications of the funding shift.

Following the suggestion from the monte--carlo experiments in jim\'enez, j. Of turbul. 2020), that dipoles are as important to the dynamics of decaying two-dimensional turbulence as individual vortex cores, it is found that the kinetic energy of this flow is carried by elongated streams formed by the concatenation of dipoles. Vortices separate into a family of small fast-moving cores, and another family of larger slowly moving ones, which can be described as `frozen' into a slowly evolving `crystal'. The kinematics of both families are very different, and only the former is self-similar. The latter is responsible for most of the kinetic energy of the flow, and its vortices form the dipoles and the streams. Mechanisms are discussed for the growth of this slow component.

Envision a world where technology has advanced to the point of creating a neural interface that seamlessly integrates with the human brain, generating an existence of unadulterated bliss. This hypothetical scenario, conceived by philosopher Robert Nozick, presents a profound dilemma: would you opt for a life of unparalleled pleasure, knowing that it would come at the cost of relinquishing your connection to reality? The Experience Machine, as Nozick termed it, is a thought-provoking concept that challenges our perceptions of the human experience. In this simulated realm, scientists have perfected a system that replicates the intricacies of real life, yet scrupulously eliminates any semblance of pain or discomfort. The trade-off, however, is absolute: in exchange for this idyllic existence, one must irrevocably sever ties with the world as we know it. The simulated experiences would be so convincingly authentic that the boundaries between reality and fantasy would become increasingly blurred. The inherent ebbs and flows of life would be supplanted by an endless succession of euphoric moments, raising a fundamental question: is a life of unmitigated pleasure truly desirable, or does the human experience rely on the interplay of contrasting emotions to be truly fulfilling?

Where does all the carbon we emit go? Carbon participates in a natural cycle that permeates all living entities: it resides in the soil, the oceans, the atmosphere, and deep underground rocks. When plants and other living organisms die, a significant portion of their stored carbon returns to the atmosphere until new plant life emerges to reabsorb it. Over millions of years, some of the carbon from ancient trees and marine life transforms into rock or fossil fuels like coal, oil, and natural gas. Natural occurrences, such as volcanic eruptions, release some of the carbon contained in these rocks. Human activities, particularly the burning of fossil fuels, also release this ancient carbon. Currently, human activities release approximately 60 times more carbon annually than all the planet's volcanoes combined.

As I endeavored to unravel the mysteries of algebra, the doorbell unexpectedly rang. Reluctantly, I set down my book to answer the door, only to find no one there. Frustrated, I returned to my studies, and just as I began to grasp a fleeting moment of clarity, the doorbell disrupted my thoughts once more. Instantly, my fragile breakthrough vanished. Once again, I went to the door, and once again, no one was there. It was exactly what a struggling math student didn't need—an unwelcome prank.

An electron is usually considered to have only one type of kinetic energy, but could it have more, for its spin and charge, or by exciting other electrons? in one dimension (1d), the physics of interacting electrons is captured well at low energies by the tomonaga-luttinger-liquid (tll) model, yet little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. Here, we report on measurements of many-body modes in 1d gated-wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. We observe two separate fermi seas at high energies, associated with spin and charge excitations, together with the emergence of three additional 1d 'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is varied by changing the amount of 1d inter-subband screening by over 45%.

In the context of Calabi-Yau orbifolds, we investigate the relationship between two distinct methods for constructing their mirror partners. Specifically, we consider an orbifold defined as the quotient of a hypersurface, represented by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial, in a weighted projective space, under the action of a suitable subgroup of phase symmetries. Two approaches have been proposed to construct the mirror partner of this orbifold. The first, known as the Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, involves a new weighted projective space and a hypersurface within it, quotiented by a dual group. In contrast, the Batyrev construction defines the mirror partner as a hypersurface in a toric variety, which is itself determined by a reflexive polytope dual to the polytope associated with the original orbifold. We provide a straightforward demonstration of the equivalence between these two seemingly disparate constructions.

New research suggests that lowering the drink-drive limit to 50mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood could save more lives than previously thought. According to a report from University College London, cutting the limit could save 65 lives a year, compared to the previous estimate of 50 lives. The study, conducted by transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, also found that the move would lead to 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads, resulting in cost savings of £119m per year. The findings are being published as MPs prepare to vote on the government's Road Safety Bill, which includes changes to speeding fines but not the drink-drive limit. The research suggests that a lower drink-drive limit could have a significant impact on road safety in the UK.

This study demonstrates that the observed narrow eccentric debris rings surrounding Fomalhaut and HD 202628 deviate significantly from predictions under conventional models of eccentric planetary perturbations (commonly termed "pericenter glow"). According to established theory, these structures originate from an initially circular, narrow planetesimal belt at semi-major axis *a*, which acquires an eccentricity *eₚ* through secular perturbations from an eccentric planet after gas disc dissipation—producing a belt with a characteristic width of *2aeₚ*. While modest adjustments to this framework allow for narrower belts through primordial eccentricity in planetesimals (potentially induced by planetary interactions during the gas-dominated protoplanetary phase), such eccentricity might alternatively stem from disc instabilities that generate eccentricity independently of planetary influence.

Chinese dam firm 'defies beijing' the china three gorges project corp is refusing to obey a government order to stop construction of one of its giant dams, the chinese state press has said. The builder of the three gorges dam is continuing work on the sister xiluodu dam, said the beijing news. The xiluodu dam is one of 30 such large-scale construction projects called to a halt because of a lack of proper environmental checks. The beijing news said the company may instead choose to pay a fine. The firm has also ignored orders to stop construction at two of its other projects - the three gorges underground power plant and the three gorges project electrical power supply plant. So far, only 22 of the 30 construction projects targeted by china's state environmental protection agency (sepa) for having not carried out mandatory environmental impact assessments have complied with its shutdown order.

The orientational dynamics within the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer—incorporating both mesogenic and functional side groups—were investigated through Kerr effect measurements and dielectric spectroscopy. This study revealed a novel finding: unlike low-molecular-weight mesogens, the electric birefringence relaxation in a mesogenic polymer melt above the nematic-isotropic transition temperature can be characterized by a superposition of multiple exponential decay processes. Among these, two dominant relaxation mechanisms were identified, which exhibit a temperature-dependent exchange of dominance across an approximately 50-degree range.  

Complementary dielectric spectroscopy further resolved two distinct orientational relaxation processes: the first arising from the rotational motion of the mesogenic side groups and the second linked to the segmental dynamics of the polymer backbone. This dual-method approach provides unprecedented insight into the hierarchical relaxation behavior of comb-shaped nematic polymers in their isotropic state.

As the subprime crisis unfolded in 2007, I was struck by how little I knew about the financial system and how unprepared I was to face a major economic shock. I had always been content to trust that the system would take care of itself, that the economy would always grow and that my job was secure. But as the crisis deepened, I realized that I had been living in a state of denial. I had a fair few stormy conversations with my wife about the matter, about the limits of the model, about my job, and on the limits of the system. Besides the crisis, I realized that I was completely wrong, that we were heading for disaster.

The experience was a wake-up call for me, a realization that I had been living in a bubble, that my understanding of the economy was limited and that I had been relying on a model that was fundamentally flawed. It was a humbling experience, one that made me realize the importance of understanding the underlying mechanics of the economy and the interconnectedness of the global financial system. It also made me more aware of the fragility of the system and the potential for future crises.
