- Some minor environmental obstacles inexplicably block movement, which feels unnecessarily restrictive.

Melzer shocks agassi second seed andre agassi suffered a comprehensive defeat by jurgen melzer in the quarter-finals of the sap open. Agassi was often bamboozled by the austrian's drop shots in san jose, losing 6-3 6-1. Defending champion and top seed andy roddick rallied to beat sweden's thomas enqvist 3-6 7-6 (8-6) 7-5. But unseeded cyril saulnier beat the fourth seed vincent spadea 6-2 6-4 and tommy haas overcame eighth seed max mirnyi 6-7 (2-7) 7-6 (7-3) 6-2. Melzer has now beaten agassi in two of their three meetings. "i had a good game plan and i executed it perfectly," he said. "it's always tough to come out to play andre. "i didn't want him to play his game. He makes you run like a dog all over the court."

When I look in the mirror today, I see a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State senator. I see all of that and a man who spent a quarter of his life in state prison—six years, to be precise—beginning as a teenager on Rikers Island for an act that nearly cost someone their life. However, what brought me from that past to my present wasn't the punishment I faced as a teenager in an adult prison or the severity of our legal system. Rather, it was the educational environment of a classroom that opened my eyes to possibilities I never imagined for myself or the justice system at large. Just a few weeks before my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a new college course being offered within the prison.

The weak gravity conjecture (wgc) demands the existence of superextremal particles in any consistent quantum theory of gravity. The standard lore is that these particles are introduced to ensure that extremal black holes are either unstable or marginally stable, but it is not clear what is wrong if this doesn't happen. This note shows that, for a generic einstein quantum theory of gravity in ads, exactly stability of extremal black branes is in tension with rigorously proven quantum information theorems about entanglement entropy. Avoiding the contradiction leads to a nonperturbative version of the wgc, which reduces to the usual statement at weak coupling. The argument is general, and it does not rely on either supersymmetry or a particular uv completion, assuming only the validity of einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holography.

In these challenging economic times, fellow TEDsters, I want to lift your spirits by sharing a remarkable, though largely overlooked, commercial success story from the past two decades. This story is comparable, in its unique way, to the achievements of tech giants like Microsoft or Google. The industry in question has managed to thrive even amid the current recession. I am referring to organized crime. While organized crime has been part of society for a long time, recent decades have seen an unprecedented surge, now representing roughly 15 percent of the global GDP. I like to call this the global shadow economy, or "McMafia" for short. But what has fueled this extraordinary growth in cross-border crime?

We investigate the transport properties and superconducting proximity effect in nSN junctions formed by a time-reversal symmetry broken Weyl semimetal (WSM) in proximity to an s-wave superconductor. Our findings reveal that the differential conductance and induced pairing amplitudes are strongly dependent on the angle between the junction direction in real space and the axis separating the Weyl nodes in momentum space. Notably, we identify the influence of a chiral chemical potential, i.e., the electron population imbalance between Weyl nodes of opposite chirality, on the transport characteristics of the junction. Remarkably, we observe a net spin polarization of Cooper pairs that are generated via Andreev reflection in the two WSM regions. The spin polarization is opposite in the two WSM regions and highly sensitive to the chirality imbalance and excitation energy.

We establish natural splittings for the values of global mackey functors at orthogonal, unitary and symplectic groups. In particular, the restriction homomorphisms between the orthogonal, unitary and symplectic groups of adjacent dimensions are naturally split epimorphisms. The interest in the splitting comes from equivariant stable homotopy theory. The equivariant stable homotopy groups of every global spectrum form a global mackey functor, so the splittings imply that certain long exact homotopy group sequences separate into short exact sequences. For the real and complex global thom spectra $\mathbf{mo}$ and $\mathbf{mu}$, the splittings imply the regularity of various euler classes related to the tautological representations of $o(n)$ and $u(n)$.

At age fourteen, my family was navigating the complex journey of adopting my younger brothers from Ethiopia. One day, my mother posed a seemingly simple question: "What date should we list as their birthday?" My initial reaction was dismissive—"The day they were born, obviously." It struck me as an absurd question—until she explained, "Kristen, neither of your brothers has a birth certificate. How would you propose we determine that?"  

That moment reshaped my understanding. Two decades later, I’m still pursuing answers—not just for my brothers, but on a global scale. The absence of birth certificates isn’t merely a personal hurdle; it’s a systemic issue affecting millions. So, why does this matter for international development? The answer lies in the invisible barriers faced by those without legal recognition—access to education, healthcare, and basic rights hinges on something as fundamental as proof of birth.

The Conservative Party has unveiled a groundbreaking proposal to revolutionize local policing, empowering communities to take a more active role in shaping law enforcement. Under this innovative plan, residents would be given the opportunity to elect their own area police commissioner, thereby fostering a more direct connection between the public and the policing authorities. This new role is designed to replace the existing, often invisible, police authorities, and would operate in tandem with the chief constable, rather than superseding their position.

However, the proposal has sparked intense debate, with the Liberal Democrats warning that it could potentially allow extremist groups to exert undue influence over policing. The Labour Party has also voiced concerns, criticizing the Conservative Party's "extravagant" promises on policing as unrealistic and lacking in substance.

Jan Berry, Chairman of the Police Federation of England and Wales, which represents the interests of rank-and-file officers, has expressed reservations about the plan, emphasizing the importance of maintaining operational independence. She cautioned that the proposed reforms could lead to policing becoming a "political football," with those holding extreme views dictating policy on the ground. Berry's concerns highlight the need for a balanced approach, one that prioritizes the needs of the community while safeguarding the integrity of the policing service.

When constructing models to summarize clinical data to be used for simulations, it is good practice to evaluate the models for their capacity to reproduce the data. This can be done by means of visual predictive checks (vpc), which consist of (1) several reproductions of the original study by simulation from the model under evaluation, (2) calculating estimates of interest for each simulated study and (3) comparing the distribution of those estimates with the estimate from the original study. This procedure is a generic method that is straightforward to apply, in general. Here we consider the application of the method to time to event data and consider the special case when a time-varying covariate is not known or cannot be approximated after event time.

Another week has flown by. Yesterday, I drove past the new house and saw that the front window was covered with boxes. Last night, I stayed up past 3:00 AM watching movies with Bethany. There's been a lot of movie watching happening here lately. Here's my recap: we watched "Thirteen" last night—a film every parent of a teenager should see. It surpassed my expectations. The Kindness of Strangers... Today I attempted to do laundry with the kids and quickly discovered how even the simplest tasks can turn into daunting ordeals. Amidst the chaos, I also found out just how wonderfully kind strangers can be, expecting nothing in return other than the joy of being kind. Believe me, it wasn't like they were trying to impress me—I was disheveled, covered in kid drool, and wearing my trusty 'laundry day' attire. Plus, let’s just say my personal hygiene wasn't at its peak, so there was no scent-based motivation either! At this moment, I'm multitasking: writing this post while watching a collection of No Doubt music videos, all from the comfort of my desk in my room.

A cross-party coalition of parliamentarians has intensified the push for reform of the House of Lords by releasing a comprehensive proposal for its transformation. The draft legislation, unveiled by the group, envisions a streamlined second chamber, with 70% of its members elected by the people. This move comes as a response to the long-standing stalemate on reform, which has persisted since 1999, when the removal of 600 hereditary peers from the House of Lords was implemented. Despite the impasse, the group remains optimistic about garnering support for the abolition of the remaining 92 hereditary peers. The government had previously shelved plans to eliminate these hereditary peers, citing the unlikelihood of success due to opposition from within the House of Lords. Prime Minister Tony Blair has consistently emphasized the need for consensus on reform, suggesting that any changes to the House of Lords, including potential modifications to its powers, may be outlined in the Labour Party's forthcoming manifesto.

Understanding how strongly correlated two-dimensional (2d) systems can give rise to unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures is one of the major unsolved problems in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2d fermi gases have emerged as clean and controllable model systems to study the interplay of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality, but direct evidence of superfluidity in these systems has been missing. Here, we demonstrate superfluidity in an ultracold 2d fermi gas by moving a periodic potential through the system and observing no dissipation below a critical velocity v$_{\rm c}$. We measure v$_{\rm c}$ as a function of interaction strength and find a maximum in the crossover regime between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our measurement establishes ultracold fermi gases as a powerful tool for studying the influence of reduced dimensionality on strongly correlated superfluids.

The estimation of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a fundamental step in most dimensionality reduction techniques. This article illustrates intrinsic, an r package that implements novel state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. In detail, the methods included in this package are the two-nn, gride, and hidalgo models. To allow these novel estimators to be easily accessible, the package contains a few high-level, intuitive functions that rely on a broader set of efficient, low-level routines. Intrinsic encompasses models that fall into two categories: homogeneous and heterogeneous intrinsic dimension estimators. The first category contains the two-nn and gride models. The functions dedicated to these two methods carry out inference under both the frequentist and bayesian frameworks. In the second category we find hidalgo, a bayesian mixture model, for which an efficient gibbs sampler is implemented.

World leaders gather to face uncertainty more than 2,000 business and political leaders from around the globe are arriving in the swiss mountain resort davos for the annual world economic forum (wef). For five days, they will discuss issues ranging from china's economic power to iraq's future after this sunday's elections. Uk prime minister tony blair and south african president thabo mbeki are among the more than 20 government leaders and heads of state leaders attending the meeting. Unlike previous years, protests against the wef are expected to be muted. Anti-globalisation campaigners have called off a demonstration planned for the weekend. The brazilian city of porto alegre will host the rival world social forum, timed to run in parallel with the wef's ritzier event in davos.

Daily health headlines bombard us with bold claims—from a single vitamin slashing heart disease risk to chocolate easing student stress, or breakthrough drugs extending lives in rare conditions. Yet these attention-grabbing statements often clash with the nuanced, incremental findings of the actual research they cite. How, then, can you discern fact from exaggeration? The key lies in scrutinizing the original studies behind the headlines. To illustrate this, we’ve crafted three hypothetical research scenarios mirroring these flashy claims. Watch as we unpack the first example, then pause to reflect on the headline’s validity. Note: these are simplified for clarity.

Gop gets scolded on recruitment naturalization ceremonies in jacksonville are to be relocated after complaints that gop activists registered new citizens as republicans. By gary fineout and alfonso chardy the citizenship ceremony in jacksonville seemed to go off as usual, with a crowd of nearly 200 people going home as new americans. Just before the new citizens left the june 29 event, an immigration official directing the swearing-in urged them to stop by a voter registration table -- a not uncommon sight at naturalization ceremonies. But this table was unusual: those handing out forms were republican volunteers -- and the party affiliation box had been checked off ahead of time to make all of the new voters members of the gop. All of it was suspicious to linda cross, who was there to watch her husband, dario cruz, take his citizenship oath.

When random effects correlate with survey design variables, conventional methods—which use individual sampling weights (calculated as inverses of inclusion probabilities) to create pseudo-likelihoods—fail to deliver asymptotically unbiased results. To address this, we introduce a novel weight-exponentiation framework for random effects distributions, ensuring unbiased inference for hyperparameter estimation.  

Our analysis shows that while frequentist techniques depend on numerical integration, only the Bayesian approach simultaneously achieves design-unbiased estimation and consistency with the population model. Through simulations and a real-world business survey application, we demonstrate the versatility of our method across diverse models and sampling schemes.  

This research culminates recent advancements integrating classical survey estimation with Bayesian modeling, bridging two historically distinct yet powerful statistical domains.

A groundbreaking report from the University College London reveals that reducing the drink-drive limit by a third could save significantly more lives than previously thought. The study, conducted by renowned transport expert Professor Richard Allsop, suggests that decreasing the limit from 80mg of alcohol per 100ml of blood to 50mg would lead to a substantial reduction in fatalities. According to the research, this change would result in 65 lives saved annually, as well as a staggering 230 fewer serious injuries on the UK's roads, compared to 2003. This reduction in road accidents would also translate to a substantial economic benefit of £119 million per annum. Interestingly, a previous estimate from a 1998 Department of Transport document had predicted that such a move could save 50 lives annually. The findings are being published as MPs prepare to vote on the government's road safety bill, which includes changes to speeding fines but not the drink-drive limit.

This configuration falls within the mid-range price bracket, and I've been able to maintain a consistent 60+ FPS on high settings with VSync enabled. In a significant blow to the spamming industry, the self-proclaimed "Spam King" of the internet has been forced to seek bankruptcy protection. Scott Richter, the mastermind behind the notorious spamming operation OptinRealBig.com, has filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy due to the mounting pressure of numerous lawsuits. The company, which has been embroiled in several high-profile legal battles, is most notably facing a multi-million dollar damages claim from Microsoft. By filing for Chapter 11, OptinRealBig aims to restructure its operations and resolve its outstanding legal issues while continuing to trade. As the third-largest spammer in the world, according to Spamhaus, a leading anti-spam watchdog, OptinRealBig has been at the center of a long-standing controversy. In December 2003, the company was sued by Microsoft and New York Attorney General Eliot Spitzer for violating anti-spam laws, specifically for sending billions of unsolicited emails through 514 compromised IP addresses in 35 countries.

Iraq is inviting bids from local, Arab, and foreign companies to bid for two telephone licenses, with the goal of significantly increasing the country's telephone coverage over the next decade. The Ministry of Communications has invited bids from companies to install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax, and internet services. The ministry aims to increase Iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate" of around 4.5% today to around 25% within the next 10 years. Additionally, the ministry hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunications sector." The bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on February 9, and the ministry is also planning a roadshow for investors in Amman, Jordan.

For nearly a quarter century, I've proudly served as a firefighter in Huntington, West Virginia. My team and I dedicate ourselves to protecting lives and property—responding to vehicle accidents, structural fires, and critical medical emergencies. As a woman leading in this traditionally male-dominated field, I've continually sought ways to enhance our community's safety. A decade ago, recognizing that modern threats extend beyond immediate crises, I pursued a nursing degree. This decision came from understanding that our greatest challenges are no longer single-incident disasters where firefighters can heroically intervene and depart. Today's complex emergencies require sustained medical expertise to address ongoing public health needs that persist long after the initial emergency response.

The direpack package aims to provide a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques as a single, consistent package in Python. The included methods fall into three categories: projection pursuit-based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust regression estimators for dimension reduction. Additionally, the package offers regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces, ranging from principal component regression to sparse partial least squares regression. Direpack also includes a variety of pre-processing utilities, such as generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting and cross-validation tools. Furthermore, the package is designed to be consistent with the scikit-learn API, allowing for seamless integration with other machine learning tools.

Jamieson issues warning to bigots scotland's justice minister has warned bigoted soccer fans that she wants to hit them "where it hurts most" by banning them from matches. Cathy jamieson said exclusion orders are one of a series of measures being considered in the scottish executive campaign against sectarianism. She praised celtic and rangers for their work in tackling the problem. However, the minister said stopping sectarian abuse associated with old firm matches is a key objective. Ms jamieson was speaking ahead of the third round scottish cup clash between the glasgow clubs at parkhead on sunday. The sectarianism long associated with sections of the support from both clubs has become a significant target for the executive. Last week ms jamieson and first minister jack mcconnell met supporters' representatives from both clubs to discuss the issue.

As of this week, EnronOnline has initiated the offering of US/Canadian dollar swaps online. In assessing a counterparty's eligibility to transact these products electronically, I conduct a thorough review of relevant documentation. This involves verifying the Financial Trading Agreement database for any specified restrictions, examining the Master Swap Agreement, and reviewing any Board resolutions received upon execution of the Master Agreement. Notably, our historical records reveal that many of the older resolutions, which were obtained to evidence authority to enter into the Master Agreement and derivative transactions, often exclude currency transactions. Given that engaging in these currency transactions with our energy customers represents a new business venture for us, it is essential that we exercise diligence in reviewing these legacy resolutions and avoid relying solely on the restrictions outlined in Lotus Notes.

I recently had the opportunity to watch Troy with my family, but unfortunately, it failed to leave a lasting impression on me. Despite its promising premise, the film's execution fell short of expectations. The lavish set design, while visually stunning, seemed to overshadow the narrative, resulting in a disjointed tone and underdeveloped themes. The retelling of the classic story lacked the emotional resonance and depth that would have made it truly compelling. Furthermore, the character development was lacking, leaving the audience without a strong emotional investment in the characters' journeys. The score, too, was a disappointment, bearing an uncanny resemblance to the iconic soundtrack of Gladiator. In contrast to Gladiator, which masterfully wove its haunting score into a gripping narrative, Troy's music felt like a pale imitation, failing to elevate the film's emotional impact.

Yukos, the Russian oil and gas company, is set to face a US court on Thursday as it continues to fight for its survival in the face of a $27 billion (£14 billion) tax bill. The company is currently undergoing a process of being broken up by Russian authorities to settle the tax debt. Yukos had previously filed for bankruptcy in the US, with the intention of using international business law to prevent the forced sale of its key oil production unit, Yuganskneftegas. However, the unit was ultimately sold for $9.4 billion to the state-owned oil firm Rosneft, following a state-led auction that was marred by controversy. Yukos' lawyers are now claiming that the auction violated US bankruptcy law, and the company and its main shareholders have vowed to take legal action against any entity that acquires its assets, using all legal means at their disposal.

Online reviews provide product evaluations for customers to make decisions. Unfortunately, the evaluations can be manipulated using fake reviews ("spams") by professional spammers, who have learned increasingly insidious and powerful spamming strategies by adapting to the deployed detectors. Spamming strategies are hard to capture, as they can be varying quickly along time, different across spammers and target products, and more critically, remained unknown in most cases. Furthermore, most existing detectors focus on detection accuracy, which is not well-aligned with the goal of maintaining the trustworthiness of product evaluations. To address the challenges, we formulate a minimax game where the spammers and spam detectors compete with each other on their practical goals that are not solely based on detection accuracy. Nash equilibria of the game lead to stable detectors that are agnostic to any mixed detection strategies.

Broadband fuels online expression fast web access is encouraging more people to express themselves online, research suggests. A quarter of broadband users in britain regularly upload content and have personal sites, according to a report by uk think-tank demos. It said that having an always-on, fast connection is changing the way people use the internet. More than five million households in the uk have broadband and that number is growing fast. The demos report looked at the impact of broadband on people's net habits. It found that more than half of those with broadband logged on to the web before breakfast. One in five even admitted to getting up in the middle of the night to browse the web. More significantly, argues the report, broadband is encouraging people to take a more active role online.

Menstruation. A simple word describing a natural biological process, weighed down by centuries of stigma, has been transformed into something most of us can only speak about in whispers. But why? as i speak to you right now, more than 800 million women around the world are having a period. None of us would exist without it, and yet it remains an "embarrassing" subject to broach. From my experience and that of the women around me, i can tell you that it's exhausting.it's exhausting to carefully take out a brown paper bag hiding a pad, stuffing it into your pocket in the middle of a class and rushing to the washroom as discreetly as possible. It's exhausting to sit through lessons and meetings pretending to be absolutely normal, while internally crying out from intense period cramps.

Investigative journalism leaves me deeply unsettled—or more precisely, the implications of this article I'm working on trouble me. My discomfort may stem from a lack of conviction, an inability to fully embrace the fervor and relentless inquiry that drives it. Growing up in a place where questions were met with bullets or imprisonment under draconian laws has conditioned me to silence. This freedom to speak openly, to challenge without fear—it feels foreign, almost dangerous. Admittedly, I’ve never been one for easy conformity.

This game is profoundly disappointing. I purchased it expecting a PC adaptation of *Katamari Damacy*, the beloved and whimsical PlayStation 2 classic, but it falls tragically short. While the core mechanic—rolling around to absorb increasingly larger objects—remains, the execution is clunky, the level design uninspired, and the overall tone unsettling. Rather than delivering playful absurdity, it evokes a bleak, almost existential dread. The grotesque portrayal of living creatures, from household pets to humans, leaves me questioning whether the developers harbored a morbid fascination with taxidermy. Far from entertaining, this experience is downright disheartening.

This game is incredibly dull. I purchased it thinking it was a PC version of 'Katamari Damacy', the beloved PS2 classic, but unfortunately, it falls far short of that expectation. The concept involves rolling up increasingly larger objects to grow bigger, but the gameplay lacks fluidity, the levels aren't creative, and the overall experience is like a bizarre horror show. It's not the entertaining kind of horror; rather, it leaves you feeling unsettled and questioning the very essence of life. Every creature in the game, from small animals to humans, feels lifeless, leading me to wonder if the developers had an interest in taxidermy.

This paper shows that the eccentric debris rings seen around the stars fomalhaut and hd 202628 are narrower than expected in the standard eccentric planet perturbation scenario (sometimes referred to as "pericenter glow"). The standard scenario posits an initially circular and narrow belt of planetesimals at semi-major axis $a$, whose eccentricity is increased to $e_f$ after the gas disc has dispersed by secular perturbations from an eccentric planet, resulting in a belt of width $2ae_f$. In a minor modification of this scenario, narrower belts can arise if the planetesimals are initially eccentric, which could result from earlier planet perturbations during the gas-rich protoplanetary disc phase. However, a primordial eccentricity could alternatively be caused by instabilities that increase the disc eccentricity, without the need for any planets.

As I gaze into the mirror today, I behold a multifaceted individual  a justice and education scholar at Columbia University, a youth mentor, an activist, and a future New York State Senator. (Cheering) I see a man who, despite spending a quarter of his life behind bars, has emerged from a tumultuous past. Six years of imprisonment, beginning at the tender age of a teenager on Rikers Island, nearly cost a man his life. However, it was not the punishment I endured as a teenager in adult prison or the harsh realities of our legal system that transformed me. Instead, it was the transformative power of a classroom that revealed to me, and indeed to our justice system as a whole, the possibility of redemption. Just a few weeks prior to my release on parole, a counselor encouraged me to enroll in a groundbreaking college course being offered within the prison walls, an opportunity that proved to be a turning point in my journey towards a brighter future.

The history of the Milky Way's assembly is gradually being uncovered, thanks to data from the Gaia mission. It appears that the globular cluster system within our galaxy has followed an equally complex formation path. To better understand this formation, we investigate the connection between these globular clusters and the known merger events that the Milky Way has undergone. By integrating Gaia's kinematic data for nearly all galactic clusters with the most comprehensive and systematically corrected sample of cluster ages, we identify clusters that likely share a common origin through an analysis of their dynamical properties, specifically within the context of integrals of motion. Our findings suggest that approximately 40% of these clusters likely originated within the galaxy itself.

What I disliked:

Deep learning has revolutionized problem-solving by tackling challenges beyond the scope of traditional heuristic approaches. Its remarkable success extends to wireless communications, despite the field's well-established theoretical foundations and existing optimal algorithms for numerous functions. While incremental improvements have been achieved by optimizing isolated receiver components, superior performance emerges when the entire receiver system is learned holistically. This comprehensive approach, however, presents a complex nonlinear optimization problem where ideal solutions remain computationally impractical. Addressing this challenge, we introduce DeepRx – an innovative fully convolutional neural network architecture that seamlessly processes the complete receiver chain from frequency-domain signal streams to decoded bits while maintaining full 5G compliance. Our novel input construction methodology, which strategically combines both data and pilot symbols, enables exceptionally precise channel estimation within the convolutional network framework.

Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor, head of the Catholic Church in England and Wales, has strongly condemned the Iraq War as a catastrophic misuse of resources that could have alleviated global poverty. In a pointed Christmas message delivered during his Midnight Mass and reiterated on BBC Radio 4's *Thought for the Day*, the Cardinal lamented how billions of pounds spent on Middle Eastern conflicts had been diverted from urgent humanitarian needs.  

Joining the Archbishop of Canterbury in voicing opposition to the war, Cardinal Murphy-O'Connor described the military expenditure as "a terrible waste" while millions worldwide suffer from extreme poverty, malnutrition, and preventable diseases. He called for 2005 to become a turning point in the fight against global inequality, urging concerted efforts to "make poverty history" through targeted campaigns and moral leadership.

 Scotland emerged victorious in their Six Nations match against Scotland at Murrayfield, as they maintained their grand slam ambitions with an impressive victory over Scotland. Despite a promising start from the Scots, a try from Hugo Southwell gave the visitors an early 8-0 lead. However, tries from Locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell, along with a third try from Denis Hickie and a third penalty from Ronan O'Gara, put the visitors in command by half-time. A second try from Jon Petrie for Scotland, however, provided some late hope, but late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy sealed the win for the Irish. With two hard-fought away victories under their belts, Eddie O'Sullivan's side can now look forward to hosting England at Lansdowne Road in two weeks' time, as they aim to secure their first Six Nations victory under their new coach, Matt Williams. Scotland, on the other hand, will look to build on their recent improvement when Italy come to Edinburgh in two weeks' time, hoping to give their coach, Franco Smith, his first Six Nations victory.

Beware of Fraudulent Tsunami Aid Website: Internet users are being cautioned to steer clear of a deceptive website falsely claiming to collect donations for tsunami victims. The site seems convincing as it mimics an outdated version of the official Disasters Emergency Committee (DEC) webpage. However, the DEC has no affiliation with this fraudulent site and has alerted the authorities about the issue. This site represents another entry in the growing list of scams exploiting the goodwill inspired by the tsunami disaster. The website's link is embedded in a spam email currently in circulation. The email is titled "Urgent Tsunami Earthquake Appeal" and is riddled with poor grammar and spelling errors, common indicators of phishing scams.

My odyssey as a polar specialist and photographer began at the tender age of four, when my family embarked on a life-changing journey from southern Canada to the remote northern reaches of Baffin Island, nestled near the icy shores of Greenland. It was there, in a small Inuit community of 200 residents, that we became one of only three non-Inuit families, fully immersed in the local culture. Devoid of modern distractions such as television, computers, and even telephones, I spent my formative years exploring the vast expanse of snow and ice, with the Inuit as my guides and mentors. This unfettered access to the harsh yet majestic polar environment ignited a deep-seated passion within me, setting the stage for a lifelong fascination with the polar regions.

Seven years ago, I graduated from Berkeley with dual degrees in Molecular and Cell Biology and Linguistics. During a career fair on campus, I secured an interview with a start-up called Theranos. At the time, there wasn't much information available about the company, but the little that was known was quite impressive. Theranos was developing a medical device that allowed for a full blood panel using just a finger-stick of blood. This innovation was intriguing not only because it eliminated the need for large needles, making the process less painful, but also because it had the potential to revolutionize predictive diagnostics.

I'm going to miss her dearly, especially now that she's heading to Bristol in September and October, and I won't see her for a while. Difference-in-differences analysis with a control group that differs considerably from a treated group is vulnerable to bias from historical events that have different effects on the groups. Constructing a more closely matched control group by matching a subset of the overall control group to the treated group may result in less bias. We study this phenomenon in simulation studies. We study the effect of mountaintop removal mining (mrm) on mortality using a difference-in-differences analysis that makes use of the increase in mrm following the 1990 clean air act amendments. For a difference-in-differences analysis of the effect of mrm on mortality, we constructed a more closely matched control group and found a 95\% confidence interval that contains substantial adverse effects along with no effect and small beneficial effects.

In 1903, U.S. President Theodore Roosevelt embarked on a historic three-day expedition into the wilderness of California’s Yosemite Valley. Amidst the ancient grandeur of towering sequoias, Roosevelt weathered a snowstorm in camp and engaged in profound fireside conversations with his renowned guide, the conservationist John Muir. While the president was celebrated for his passion for nature, Muir’s invitation carried deeper urgency—Yosemite faced imminent peril.  

Despite being designated protected land in 1864, the valley remained vulnerable to unchecked development by the early 20th century. It stood at the center of a prolonged national debate over balancing wilderness preservation with public accessibility—a vision far more complex in practice than in principle. The roots of this struggle traced back to the 1849 Gold Rush, when waves of prospectors descended upon the Sierra Nevada foothills, forever altering the region’s destiny.

Introducing a Novel Mixed Variable Symplectic Integrator for Planetary Systems: Enhanced Stability and Efficiency in Close Encounter Simulations

A groundbreaking mixed variable symplectic (MVS) integrator has been developed to accurately model planetary systems, particularly those involving close encounters. By leveraging a time regularisation technique, this innovative method successfully maintains the stability properties inherent to symplectic integrators while adaptively reducing the effective step size during planetary interactions. This approach enables the utilisation of high-order MVS schemes, facilitating large time steps in regions far from close encounters. Notably, the algorithm demonstrates exceptional performance in resolving near-collision events, characterised by mutual separations of mere fractions of the physical radius, without compromising the time-step size typically employed in weakly perturbed systems, such as our solar system. The long-term behaviour of the integrator is showcased through simulations of six super-Earths undergoing intense scattering over a period of 50,000 years. Comparative analyses with hybrid methods, including the Mercury algorithm, reveal that the proposed MVS integrator achieves superior energy conservation at equivalent computational costs.

The ache of my sister's absence is palpable today, a poignant reminder of the void she leaves behind. Though her visit was brief, her presence was a profound one, imbuing our time together with a sense of warmth and connection. As I delve into the scrapbook of our Tasmanian adventure, memories of our recent escapade come flooding back, and I am met with a mix of nostalgia and longing. The impending distance between us, with her impending move to Bristol, weighs heavily on my heart. Her presence served as a tangible link to my family, a connection that now feels tenuous and far away. The pang of homesickness is overwhelming, and I find myself yearning for the comfort and familiarity of loved ones. As the new term dawns tomorrow, I am left wondering how the holidays slipped away with such haste, leaving me with a sense of disorientation and melancholy.

I was thoroughly impressed with the Assassin's Creed series, which excelled in its execution on multiple fronts, including ship battles, sailing, hunting, and parkour, among others. As I delved deeper into the story, I found myself becoming increasingly attached to the protagonist, whose character development was well-crafted. The narrative begins with a rocky start, but it gradually improves as the game progresses, much like every action-adventure game I've played. Edward, as a character, was particularly intriguing, and so were the supporting characters. I felt that each collectible item had a tangible purpose in the game, which is something I never experienced in previous games. Perhaps this is because I sensed that they played a more significant role in the story, even if they weren't essential to the main plot. They were enjoyable to collect and were rewarded in a well-balanced manner.

Electric Network Frequency (ENF) fluctuations serve as a highly effective tool in multimedia forensics. We introduce an advanced method for ENF estimation that incorporates temporal windowing through the filter-bank Capon spectral estimator. This technique leverages the Gohberg-Semencul factorization to handle the Toeplitz structure inherent in the model's covariance matrix. Notably, for the first time in ENF analysis, a non-rectangular temporal window is utilized prior to spectral estimation, enhancing the method's versatility. Additionally, Krylov matrices are utilized to expedite matrix inversions. This innovative approach surpasses state-of-the-art methods in terms of ENF estimation, particularly when implementing a brief one-second time window for power recordings. In the context of speech recordings, our method achieves remarkable accuracy while maintaining computational efficiency.

Cabinet anger at brown cash raid ministers are unhappy about plans to use whitehall cash to keep council tax bills down, local government minister nick raynsford has acknowledged. Gordon brown reallocated £512m from central to local government budgets in his pre-budget report on thursday. Mr raynsford said he had held some "pretty frank discussions" with fellow ministers over the plans. But he said local governments had to deliver good services without big council tax rises. The central government cash is part of a £1bn package to help local authorities in england keep next year's council tax rises below 5%, in what is likely to be a general election year. Mr raynsford said nearly all central government departments had an interest in well run local authorities.

Processing spontaneous conversations from real-world settings—particularly child-centered audio recordings—poses significant challenges for speech recognition systems. However, developing robust models capable of handling such diverse acoustic conditions holds immense value for both language acquisition research and clinical applications. These models can help quantify and analyze the speech input children receive and produce, while also aiding in early diagnosis and intervention assessment.  

In this work, we introduce an open-source neural network designed to classify audio segments into four key categories: vocalizations from the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations from other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To ensure broad applicability, we compiled a multilingual dataset comprising 260 hours of child-centered recordings across 10 languages, providing a rich foundation for model training and evaluation.

This weekend was a truly exceptional experience. Following a prolonged hiatus, I dedicated a significant amount of time to indulging in a literary marathon, devoting myself to the world of reading. I devoured three captivating novels - Bruce Sterling's 'Heavy Weather', Larry Niven's 'World of Ptavvs', and Octavia Butler's 'Parable of the Sower'. 'Heavy Weather' transported me to a dystopian near-future United States ravaged by the devastating effects of global warming and societal chaos. Although the novel was expertly crafted, with a well-paced narrative and vividly rendered characters, I must admit that I didn't find myself deeply invested in the story or its protagonists. The novel was enjoyable, but it lacked a profound emotional resonance, leaving me with a sense of mild satisfaction rather than a lasting impression.

Orientational dynamics in the isotropic phase of a comb-shaped nematic polymer with mesogenic and functional side groups was studied using the kerr effect and dielectric spectroscopy. For the first time, it was found that in a mesogenic polymer, in contrast to low-molecular-weight mesogens, the relaxation of the electric birefringence of a melt above the temperature of the nematic-isotropic phase transition can be presented by a sum of several exponential processes, two of which play a decisive role. These main processes replace each other in a temperature range of about fifty degrees. Dielectric spectroscopy also made it possible to distinguish two processes of orientational relaxation: the first is due to rotation of the side mesogenic groups, and the second is associated with motion of the main chain segments.

Ranking algorithms play a crucial role in online platforms ranging from search engines to recommender systems. In this paper, we identify a surprising consequence of popularity-based rankings: the fewer the items reporting a given signal, the higher the share of the overall traffic they collectively attract. This few-get-richer effect emerges in settings where there are few distinct classes of items (e.g., left-leaning news sources versus right-leaning news sources), and items are ranked based on their popularity. We demonstrate analytically that the few-get-richer effect emerges when people tend to click on top-ranked items and have heterogeneous preferences for the classes of items. Using simulations, we analyze how the strength of the effect changes with assumptions about the setting and human behavior. We also test our predictions experimentally in an online experiment with human participants. Our findings have important implications to understand the spread of misinformation.

Causal inference from observational data is the goal of many data analyses in the health and social sciences. However, academic statistics has often frowned upon data analyses with a causal objective. The introduction of the term "data science" provides a historic opportunity to redefine data analysis in such a way that it naturally accommodates causal inference from observational data. Like others before, we organize the scientific contributions of data science into three classes of tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (which includes causal inference). An explicit classification of data science tasks is necessary to discuss the data, assumptions, and analytics required to successfully accomplish each task. We argue that a failure to adequately describe the role of subject-matter expert knowledge in data analysis is a source of widespread misunderstandings about data science.

Reasoning about graphs evolving over time is a challenging concept in many domains, such as bioinformatics, physics, and social networks. We consider a common case in which edges can be short term interactions (e.g., messaging) or long term structural connections (e.g., friendship). In practice, long term edges are often specified by humans. Human-specified edges can be both expensive to produce and suboptimal for the downstream task. To alleviate these issues, we propose a model based on temporal point processes and variational autoencoders that learns to infer temporal attention between nodes by observing node communication. As temporal attention drives between-node feature propagation, using the dynamics of node interactions to learn this key component provides more flexibility while simultaneously avoiding issues associated with human-specified edges.

Dear Global Citizens,

We are confronted with a crisis of unparalleled magnitude. Please prepare to receive a statement from António Guterres, the Secretary-General of the United Nations. 

The climate emergency is the defining challenge of our era. We find ourselves in a race against time, and unfortunately, we are falling behind. There is an increasing sense of urgency and impatience, particularly among the youth, due to the lack of decisive global action. It is crucial that we see greater ambition from all sectors: governments, cities, businesses, investors, and individuals alike. 

I am encouraged by the launch of TED Countdown. Your influence and innovative ideas can play a pivotal role in accelerating progress towards achieving a carbon-neutral world by 2050. This is our only viable path to mitigating the worst impacts of global warming. We possess the tools, scientific knowledge, and resources necessary to succeed. Now, let us engage in this race with renewed political resolve and vigor.

In the autumn of 1974, I embarked on what felt like my 13th attempt to conquer the second chapter of my high school algebra textbook. As a self-proclaimed non-math whiz, I detest mathematics. In fact, I am math-aphobic. You can imagine my frustration. As I wrestled to decipher the complexities of algebra, the doorbell rang, interrupting my struggle. I set my book aside and went to answer the door, only to find it was an empty gesture. I returned to my studies, just as I was on the cusp of grasping a glimmer of understanding, the doorbell rang once more. This time, my epiphany vanished as suddenly as it had appeared. I opened the door, only to find it was another prank, leaving me, a struggling math student, feeling bewildered and bemused.

The Weak Gravity Conjecture (WGC) posits that any viable quantum theory of gravity must incorporate superextremal particles. Conventionally, these particles are believed to prevent extremal black holes from achieving absolute stability—though the precise consequences of violating this condition remain unclear. In this work, we demonstrate that within a broad class of Einsteinian quantum gravity theories in anti-de Sitter (AdS) space, perfect stability of extremal black branes fundamentally conflicts with established quantum information theorems concerning entanglement entropy. Resolving this paradox yields a nonperturbative formulation of the WGC, which naturally converges to its weak-coupling counterpart. Our framework is universally applicable, requiring neither supersymmetry nor specific ultraviolet completions—only the foundational principles of Einsteinian gravity, effective field theory, and holography remain essential.

As an avid enthusiast of politics, I was initially thrilled to discover a game that promised to simulate the journey to the presidency. The concept seemed incredibly engaging, and I eagerly anticipated immersing myself in the experience. However, my enthusiasm was short-lived. Upon launching the game, I was presented with a binary choice: affiliate with either the Republican or Democratic party. The absence of independent or third-party options was somewhat disheartening, but I chose to proceed, hoping to uncover more depth and complexity. One redeeming aspect of the game is its character customization feature, albeit limited in scope. This aspect allows for a degree of creativity, even permitting the unusual option of campaigning with or without one's head – a quirky touch that adds a hint of humor to the experience.

Online reviews are crucial for customers when it comes to making informed purchase decisions. Unfortunately, these reviews can be compromised by fake reviews or "spams" inserted by professional spammers. These spammers continue to develop more sophisticated and powerful tactics to evade existing detection methods. Capturing these spamming strategies is challenging as they evolve rapidly, vary between spammers and products, and are often unknown. Moreover, most current detection techniques prioritize accuracy but do not necessarily uphold the reliability of product evaluations. To solve these issues, we propose a minimax game framework where spammers and spam detectors tactically compete, focusing on pragmatic objectives beyond mere detection accuracy. The Nash equilibrium of this game yields stable detection systems that are robust against any combination of detection methods.

El Paso Natural Gas has launched an open season to gauge market interest in a potential mainline expansion, a move that aims to alleviate the pressing capacity constraints at the California border. This development comes on the heels of a previous open season, which saw an overwhelming response with bids for 14.4 billion cubic feet per day (bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity, far exceeding the initial offering of 1.2 bcf/d. The pipeline has been facing intense scrutiny from regulators and shippers, who have been urging El Paso to increase its capacity to meet the surging demand and skyrocketing prices in California. In a recent letter, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission's (FERC) Office of Energy Projects, emphasized the need for El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to help mitigate the current challenges plaguing the California gas market.

Last night was particularly frustrating. I ended up discarding the sweater I was working on, even though I had completed about 14 inches. I lost approximately $10 worth of yarn, with each skein costing $8. Unfortunately, by the time I reached the armholes, there were too many stitches, and the mohair kept tangling as I knitted. Trying to undo the rows, known as frogging, was ineffective because the yarn frequently snapped. Despite my usual tendency to swear in such situations, I remained surprisingly calm. Instead, I just listened to the second season of "Sex and the City" (thanks to my library) while I unraveled my work in a rather gloomy manner. Eventually, I grew tired and decided to throw it away. Looking back, I guess I can say I learned a lot from attempting to make this sweater.

Entanglement is a fundamental component in quantum information processing, and its detection is crucial for harnessing its power. A widely employed method for identifying entanglement is through the use of entanglement witnesses, which are operators that yield non-negative measurement outcomes for all separable states, while potentially producing negative results for certain entangled states. However, constructing an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled systems poses significant challenges due to the exponential growth of the Hilbert space dimension with respect to system size. In contrast, for a specific class of n-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, a robust witness scheme has been developed, requiring n+1 local measurement settings and capable of withstanding up to 50% white noise contamination. Notably, a more efficient witness for GHZ-like states has also been proposed, necessitating only two local measurement settings and tolerating up to 33% white noise, thereby offering a more practical solution for entanglement detection in these systems.

Recent breakthroughs in optomechanics and electromechanics have enabled the preparation of macroscopic mechanical oscillators in a range of quantum states, including the quantum ground state, quadrature squeezed states, and entangled states of motion. This capability has not only pushed the boundaries of quantum behavior to larger, more tangible objects, but also sparked innovative ideas for harnessing mechanical oscillators in quantum information processing and communication, as well as in the development of precision force sensors that transcend the standard quantum limit. However, the inherently linear interaction between motion and light or electricity has limited access to a broader spectrum of quantum states of motion, such as cat states and energy squeezed states. To overcome this limitation, early optomechanical proposals envisioned the creation of strong quadratic coupling between motion and light, which would unlock a wider range of quantum states. While experimental demonstrations of quadratically coupled optomechanical systems have been successful, they have yet to achieve the milestone of accessing nonclassical states of motion.

The aftermath of returning from practicum proved challenging, with the first week being particularly difficult. However, by the second week, clarity began to emerge. What became evident is that post-practicum trauma is a widespread yet overlooked issue only now gaining recognition.  

Initially overwhelmed, I found relief after meeting with the course coordinator, where I shared my concerns. This not only lightened my burden but also helped prevent another student from facing the same pedagogically inadequate practicum environment.  

Just yesterday, I encountered a distressed classmate who was adamant about warning the next student assigned to her problematic school. I reassured her and suggested we first address the issue with the administration rather than acting impulsively.

Virgin radio offers 3g broadcast uk broadcaster virgin radio says it will become the first station in the world to offer radio via 3g mobiles. The radio station, in partnership with technology firm sydus, will broadcast on selected 2g and high-speed 3g networks. Later this year listeners will be able to download software from the virgin website which enables the service. James cridland, head of new media at virgin radio, said: "it places radio at the heart of the 3g revolution." virgin radio will be the first station made available followed by two digital stations, virgin radio classic rock and virgin radio groove. Mr cridland said: "this application will enable anyone, anywhere to listen to virgin radio simply with the phone in their pocket.

The proposals outlining policing plans for local communities would require residents to elect their own area police commissioners, under plans unveiled by the Conservative Party. Party leader Michael Howard stated that the new role would replace "inefficient" police authorities. He clarified that the new office would not supersede the role of a Chief Constable. The Liberal Democrats, however, expressed concerns that the plan could lead to extreme groups dominating policing, while Labour criticized the "extravagant" Conservative promises on policing. Responding to the plans, the Chairman of the Police Federation of England and Wales, which represents rank-and-file officers, emphasized the importance of retaining operational independence. Jan Berry, the Chairman, warned  "It is a service, not a political football to be kicked around every time an election approaches. These plans could result in those with extreme political views dictating what actually happens on the ground.

In our recent class discussion, we delved into the assigned reading for the week, specifically Edward S. Casey's seminal work, "Getting Back into Place  Toward a Renewed Study of the Place-World." The first chapter, "Implacement," and the subsequent "Displacement," offer a thought-provoking exploration of the concept of time as the dominant force shaping our contemporary world and culture. Casey skillfully argues that our society's emphasis on time as the situating force has led to a disconnection from the notion of place as the central axis of our cultural identity. He posits that by reconceptualizing place as the foundation of culture, we can cultivate a more grounded and community-driven existence. This reading has been the most compelling thus far in our course, and I must admit that it has left a lasting impression on me.

More women turn to net security older people and women are increasingly taking charge of protecting home computers against malicious net attacks, according to a two-year study. The number of women buying programs to protect pcs from virus, spam and spyware attacks rose by 11.2% each year between 2002 and 2004. The study, for net security firm preventon, shows that security messages are reaching a diversity of surfers. It is thought that 40% of those buying home net security programs are retired. For the last three years, that has gone up by an average of 13.2%. But more retired women (53%) were buying security software than retired men. The research reflects the changing stereotype and demographics of web users, as well as growing awareness of the greater risks that high-speed broadband net connections can pose to surfers.

Two hours into this game, and I'm utterly captivated—both by its brilliance and its relentless humor. The gameplay mechanics are polished, the art direction is stunning, and the soundtrack is unexpectedly refined, perfectly complementing the experience. The narrative and world-building are engaging, while the puzzles strike a satisfying balance of challenge and enjoyment.  

Yet, what truly elevates this game above the rest is its masterful comedy. Whether you're a seasoned RPG veteran—familiar with every trope since the genre's inception—or a complete newcomer, the clever jokes, sharp parodies, and nostalgic references will leave you in stitches. Fans of *Final Fantasy VII* or *X*, in particular, will find it irresistible (though I won't spoil why).  

In fact, after just two hours, I had to pause—not out of frustration, but because my cheeks ached from laughing so hard. This game is an absolute gem, and I can't wait to dive back in.

The Chancellor, Gordon Brown, has stated that the focus of the upcoming election campaign will centre on the economy's future, with Labour's poll strategy. This comes after a meeting at Number 10 Downing Street, where cabinet members gathered to discuss the election. Brown emphasized that voters will recognize that Labour has brought stability and growth, and will continue to do so in the future. Meanwhile, the Conservatives have outlined their plans to tackle the 'yob culture' issue, and the Liberal Democrats have provided more details about their proposals to replace council tax. The Archbishop of Canterbury has written an open letter to all three parties, urging them not to turn the election into a competition about who can most effectively frighten voters about terrorism, asylum, and crime. He called on them not to exploit people's fears in this election.

The CFTC has approved new NYMEX Rule 6.40C, which allows futures commission merchants to use their own internal, proprietary systems to generate average price ("AP") calculations when multiple prices are received on an order or series of orders. The rule is effective as of October 3, 2000.
The notice informs NYMEX and COMEX members that they should be aware of the new rule and its effective date. The rule allows FCMs to use their own internal, proprietary systems to calculate APs under certain conditions specified in the rule. This may provide greater flexibility for FCMs in their operations and may allow them to more efficiently calculate APs for their customers.

Spontaneous conversations in real-world settings such as those found in child-centered recordings have been shown to be amongst the most challenging audio files to process. Nevertheless, building speech processing models handling such a wide variety of conditions would be particularly useful for language acquisition studies in which researchers are interested in the quantity and quality of the speech that children hear and produce, as well as for early diagnosis and measuring effects of remediation. In this paper, we present our approach to designing an open-source neural network to classify audio segments into vocalizations produced by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations produced by other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To this end, we gathered diverse child-centered corpora which sums up to a total of 260 hours of recordings and covers 10 languages.

As the UK prepares for a highly anticipated general election, the nation's economic future is poised to take centre stage in Labour's campaign, according to Chancellor Gordon Brown. This assertion comes on the heels of the final cabinet meeting at 10 Downing Street before the expected election announcement. Brown expressed confidence that voters will acknowledge Labour's track record of fostering stability and growth, and that the party will continue to build upon this momentum. In a parallel development, the Conservative Party unveiled its strategy to combat anti-social behaviour, while the Liberal Democrats provided further insight into their proposals for replacing council tax. In a timely intervention, the Archbishop of Canterbury penned an open letter to the three main parties, cautioning against exploiting the electorate's fears during the campaign. He urged politicians to refrain from transforming the election into a competition centred on who can most effectively instil fear in voters regarding terrorism, asylum, and crime.

On October 4, 1957, the world witnessed a historic moment as the Soviet Union launched Sputnik, the first man-made satellite, into space. This small metal ball, measuring less than two feet in diameter, ignited a space race between the United States and the Soviet Union that would last for eighteen years and reshape the global landscape. Sputnik was not the first human-made technology to reach space, however. That distinction belongs to the V-2 rocket, used by Germany in futile attempts to attack Allied cities during the final years of World War II. Although the V-2 rocket was not particularly effective, both the United States and the Soviet Union seized the technology and scientists behind it after the war and integrated them into their own space programs.

Sustainability represents the what, the where and the how of what is caught. The who and the why are what's important to me. I want to know the people behind my dinner choices. I want to know how i impact them. I want to know how they impact me. I want to know why they fish. I want to know how they rely on the water's bounty for their living. Understanding all of this enables us to shift our perception of seafood away from a commodity to an opportunity to restore our ecosystem. It allows for us to celebrate the seafood that we're also so fortunate to eat.so what do we call this? i think we call it restorative seafood. Where sustainability is the capacity to endure and maintain, restorative is the ability to replenish and progress.

Here, we report the observation of thermally driven transverse vibrations in a photonic crystal waveguide, comprising two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap. Theoretical models are developed and validated to describe the transduction of beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode of the waveguide for probe frequencies both near and far from the dielectric band edge. This research provides a foundation for evaluating the potential detrimental effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of photonic crystal waveguides, which have been designed for near-field atom trapping. Ultimately, our long-term goal is to establish strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the guided modes of the waveguide, thereby enabling optomechanics at the quantum level, harnessing the synergy between atoms, photons, and phonons.

The pursuit of causal insights from observational data is a cornerstone of research endeavors in the health and social sciences. Historically, however, the academic statistics community has been skeptical of analyses aimed at establishing causality. The emergence of data science presents a unique opportunity to redefine the paradigm of data analysis, seamlessly integrating causal inference from observational data. By categorizing data science contributions into three distinct task classes – descriptive analytics, predictive modeling, and counterfactual prediction (encompassing causal inference) – we establish a framework for systematically addressing the data requirements, assumptions, and analytical methodologies essential to each task. A clear taxonomy of data science tasks is crucial for clarifying the interplay between data, assumptions, and analytics, and for highlighting the pivotal role of domain expertise in data analysis – a factor often overlooked, leading to widespread misconceptions about the data science discipline.

Two major U.S. companies have initiated advertising campaigns to "set the record straight" regarding their products and corporate practices. Walmart, the world's largest retailer, has responded to critics by purchasing more than 100 full-page ads in national newspapers. This effort aims to address concerns about its pay, benefits, and promotion strategies. Similarly, pharmaceutical company Eli Lilly is launching a campaign to counter "false" claims about its product, Prozac. Walmart began its initiative with ads in newspapers such as The Wall Street Journal, featuring an open letter from company president Lee Scott stating it was time for the public to hear the "unfiltered truth." "There are many 'urban legends' circulating these days about Walmart, but facts are facts.

We investigate the transport characteristics and the superconducting proximity effect in NSN junctions composed of a Weyl semimetal with broken time-reversal symmetry, positioned near an s-wave superconductor. Our study reveals that both the differential conductance and the induced pairing amplitudes are significantly influenced by the angle between the direction of the junction in real space and the axis that separates the Weyl nodes in momentum space. We explore how a chiral chemical potential—representing the electron population imbalance between Weyl nodes of opposite chirality—affects the junction's transport properties. Notably, we discover a net spin polarization of Cooper pairs generated through Andreev reflection within the two Weyl semimetal regions. This spin polarization is opposite in each of the Weyl semimetal regions and is notably sensitive to the chirality imbalance and excitation energy.

Seven years after earning dual degrees in Molecular and Cell Biology and Linguistics from UC Berkeley, I attended a campus career fair that led to an interview with a biotech startup called Theranos. At the time, publicly available information about the company was scarce, but what little existed appeared groundbreaking. The company was developing a revolutionary medical device capable of performing comprehensive blood tests using just a finger-prick sample. This innovation promised to eliminate the need for traditional venous blood draws, offering both a less invasive patient experience and the potential to transform diagnostic medicine through predictive analytics.

Prince was crowned the top music earner in 2004, outperforming other pop legends such as Madonna and Elton John in US Magazine's annual list. The singer generated a staggering $56.5 million (£30.4 million) from concerts, album, and publishing sales with his successful Musicology Tour and album. He maintained his lead over Madonna, who earned $54.9 million (£29.5 million) during her global re-invention tour. Veteran duo Simon and Garfunkel placed 10th, with their comeback tour contributing $24.9 million (£13.4 million) to their earnings last year. Prince's triumphant return to the stage after a decade in the commercial wilderness was hailed by the magazine, with his 2004 tour grossing a remarkable $90.3 million (£48.5 million) in ticket sales and selling 1.9 million copies of his critically acclaimed album Musicology.

We propose that an electron, typically regarded as possessing a single type of kinetic energy, may actually exhibit multiple types of kinetic energy, owing to its spin and charge, or through the excitation of other electrons. In one dimension, the physics of interacting electrons is well-described by the Tomonaga-Luttinger liquid (TLL) model at low energies. However, little has been observed experimentally beyond this linear regime. In this work, we report on the measurement of many-body modes in one-dimensional gated wires using a tunnelling spectroscopy technique. Our results reveal the presence of two distinct Fermi seas at high energies, corresponding to spin and charge excitations, as well as the emergence of three additional one-dimensional'replica' modes that strengthen with decreasing wire length. The effective interaction strength in the wires is modulated by varying the amount of one-dimensional inter-subband screening by over 45%.

Andrea Levy, a previous Orange Prize winner, has garnered the Whitbread Novel of the Year award for her book "Small Island." This victory positions her as the leading contender for the overall prize, having surpassed Booker Prize recipient Alan Hollinghurst's "The Line of Beauty." On another note, Geraldine McCaughrean has triumphed in the children's fiction category for the third time with "Not the End of the World." Each category winner progresses to compete for the Whitbread Book of the Year title, which offers a £25,000 prize. A distinguished panel of judges, including Sir Trevor McDonald, actor Hugh Grant, and author Joanne Harris, will convene on January 25 to select the overall winner, with the announcement to follow that evening. According to bookmaker William Hill, Levy's novel, based in London, is favored to win with 6/4 odds.

Entanglement serves as a fundamental resource in quantum information science. Among the various methods for identifying entangled states, entanglement witnesses stand out as a practical approach—these operators yield non-negative expectation values for all separable states while potentially detecting negativity for specific entangled states. However, constructing efficient entanglement witnesses for general multipartite systems remains highly challenging due to the exponential growth of Hilbert space dimensionality with increasing particle number.  

For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most noise-resistant witness requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and remains effective even with up to 50% white noise contamination. In contrast, the most resource-efficient witness for such states demands only two measurement settings but has a reduced noise tolerance, remaining valid for up to 33% white noise.

We introduce a novel skyrme-like conserved current in the effective theory of pions and vector mesons based on the idea of hidden local symmetry. The associated charge is equivalent to the skyrmion charge for any smooth configuration. In addition, there exist singular configurations that can be identified as n_f=1 baryons charged under the new symmetry. Under this identification, the vector mesons play the role of the chern-simons vector fields living on the quantum hall droplet that forms the n_f=1 baryon. We propose that this current is the correct effective expression for the baryon current at low energies. This proposal gives a unified picture for the two types of baryons and allows them to continuously transform one to the other in a natural way. In addition, chern-simons dualities on the droplet can be interpreted as a result of seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

This text introduces novel concepts and perspectives, as well as challenges, in measuring and controlling open quantum systems through sequential methods. It explores how protocols that utilize both repeated quantum measurements and dynamical decoupling control pulses can: (i) safeguard and confine quantum dynamics against decoherence in alignment with Zeno physics; (ii) provide an analytical prediction for the likelihood of transferring a quantum system into a targeted quantum state via stochastic sequential measurements; and (iii) optimally reconstruct the spectral density of environmental noise by orthogonalizing filter functions in the frequency domain for a designed quantum sensor. Accomplishing these objectives will significantly enhance our ability to observe and manipulate open quantum systems, leading to progress in quantum science and technology.

Advances in high-resolution optical imaging have been significantly driven by the widespread adoption of nonlinear fluorescence emission techniques. This study focuses on the anomalous saturation behavior of nitrogen vacancy defects in diamond, a phenomenon that has garnered considerable attention in recent years. Notably, our experiments revealed a pronounced reduction in fluorescence emission when subjected to high-power laser excitation, which in turn amplified the nonlinearity of the fluorescence signal and altered the spatial frequency distribution of the resulting fluorescence image. To extract high spatial frequency information, we employed a differential excitation protocol that involved modulating the power of the excitation laser. This approach yielded a substantial improvement in spatial resolution, with an approximate 1.6-fold enhancement compared to conventional confocal microscopy. Given the relative simplicity of the experimental setup and data processing requirements, we anticipate that this method will have far-reaching implications for enhancing the spatial resolution of sensing and biological labeling applications that utilize defects in solid-state materials.

Brizzel to run aaa's in sheffield ballymena sprinter paul brizzel will be among eight of ireland's european indoor hopefuls competing in this weekend's aaa's championships.
