Entanglement is a key resource for quantum information processing. A widely used tool for detecting entanglement is entanglement witness, which measures the witness operator and is guaranteed to be positive for separable states and can be negative for entangled states. In reality, due to the exponentially increasing Hilbert space dimension with respect to the system size, it is very challenging to construct an efficient entanglement witness for general multipartite entangled states. For $n$-partite Greenberger-Horne-Zeilinger (GHZ)-like states, the most robust witness scheme requires $n+1$ local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/2$ white noise. As a comparison, the most efficient witness for GHZ-like states only needs two local measurement settings and can tolerate up to $1/3$ white noise.

We are often viewed as the last resort for addressing failed social policies. We cannot choose who comes to us or dictate how long they remain. We are left to manage individuals for whom all other social safety nets have failed—those who have slipped through the cracks. Other systems can't handle them, so we must. Our task is to contain and control them. Over the years, as part of the prison system, our nation, and society, we have become adept at this. However, this is not an achievement to take pride in. Currently, we incarcerate more people per capita than any other nation in the world. Moreover, there are more Black men in prison today than there were enslaved in 1850.

Our research investigates the transport characteristics and superconducting proximity effects in nsn junctions composed of a time-reversal symmetry-broken Weyl semimetal (WSM) coupled with an s-wave superconductor. The analysis reveals a pronounced dependence of both differential conductance and induced pairing amplitudes on the angular alignment between the junction's spatial orientation and the momentum-space axis connecting the Weyl nodes. Notably, we demonstrate how a chiral chemical potential—manifesting as an electron population imbalance between oppositely-chiral Weyl nodes—significantly impacts the junction's transport behavior. A particularly striking finding is the emergence of net spin polarization in Cooper pairs generated through Andreev reflection within both WSM regions. This polarization exhibits opposite orientations in the two regions and demonstrates exceptional sensitivity to both chirality imbalance and excitation energy levels.

Israeli club look to africa four african players, including zimbabwe goalkeeper energy murambadoro, are all ready to play for israeli club hapoel bnei sakhnin in the uefa cup. Bnei sakhnin are the first arab side ever to play in european competition and will play english premiership side newcastle united in the first round. Warriors' goalkeeper murambadoro, who made a name for himself at the african nations cup finals in tunisia, helped bnei sakhnin overcome albania's partizani tirana 6-1 in the previous round. Murambadoro moved to israel recently after a brief stint with south african club hellenic. The club won the israeli cup final last season and are based in sakhnin, which is near haifa. The club have a strong ethic and are high profile promoters of peace and co-operation within israel.

England’s rugby union captain, Jason Robinson, has set his sights on securing a historic double victory against Australia this Saturday. Robinson, who transitioned from rugby league to union in 2000, will lead England against the Wallabies at Twickenham in a highly anticipated clash at 14:30 GMT. Later that day, at 18:15 GMT, Great Britain’s rugby league team will face Australia in the Tri-Nations final.  

"Defeating Australia in both matches would be an extraordinary accomplishment, particularly for rugby league," Robinson remarked.  

England, fresh from consecutive autumn international triumphs over Canada and South Africa, have an opportunity to claim their third straight win while avenging their crushing 51-15 defeat to Australia in June. Meanwhile, Great Britain could break a 34-year drought against the Australians with a victory at Elland Road.

In the realm of effective field theories for pions and vector mesons, we propose a new conserved current akin to the Skyrme model, built upon the concept of hidden local symmetry. The charge associated with this current matches the Skyrmion charge in any smooth field configuration. Moreover, there are singular configurations that can be interpreted as baryons with flavor number n_f=1, exhibiting charge under this new symmetry. Within this framework, vector mesons act as Chern–Simons vector fields existing on a quantum Hall droplet, which constitutes the n_f=1 baryon. We suggest that this current accurately represents the baryon current at low energies. Our model offers a cohesive perspective that reconciles both baryon types, enabling a natural transformation between them. Additionally, Chern-Simons dualities on the droplet can be understood through a Seiberg-like duality between gluons and vector mesons.

Groundbreaking ideas, fresh perspectives, and innovative challenges in harnessing sequential schemes to measure and control open quantum systems are unveiled. By leveraging protocols that combine repeated quantum measurements with dynamical decoupling control pulses, we demonstrate the potential to: (i) harness the Zeno effect to shield and preserve quantum dynamics from decoherence, (ii) develop analytical models to predict the likelihood of successfully transferring a quantum system to a target state through stochastic sequential measurements, and (iii) optimize the reconstruction of environmental noise spectra by orthogonalizing filter functions in the frequency domain, thereby enhancing the performance of quantum sensors. The successful accomplishment of these objectives will significantly expand our capacity to observe, manipulate, and understand open quantum systems, driving progress in quantum science and technology.

We are the organization that serves as the safety net for those who have fallen through the cracks of our social systems. We provide a haven for individuals who have exhausted all other social safety nets and are left with nowhere else to turn. We accept anyone who needs our help, regardless of their background or circumstances. Our mission is to provide a sense of stability and control for those who are struggling, over the years, as a society, we have developed a sophisticated system for managing these individuals, but this should not be a source of pride. In fact, the United States has one of the highest rates of incarceration per capita in the world. We have more black men behind bars today than were enslaved in 1850. This is a sobering statistic that highlights the need for us to re-examine our approach to criminal justice and to work towards a more just and compassionate society.

The proliferation of online platforms, from search engines to personalized recommendation systems, relies heavily on the efficacy of ranking algorithms. A striking phenomenon has been observed in the context of popularity-driven rankings, where a smaller number of items signaling a particular attribute collectively garner a disproportionately large share of overall traffic. This "few-get-richer" phenomenon manifests in environments characterized by a limited number of distinct item categories, such as polarized news sources, where items are ranked according to their popularity. Through analytical modeling, we establish that this effect arises when users exhibit a propensity to engage with top-ranked items and display heterogeneous preferences for different item categories. We employ simulation-based analyses to investigate how the magnitude of this effect varies in response to changes in environmental assumptions and human behavior. Furthermore, we validate our theoretical predictions through a controlled online experiment involving human participants. The findings of this study have significant implications for understanding the dynamics of misinformation dissemination in online ecosystems.

Our story begins in 1964, when a 23-year-old Bob Dylan stands at the precipice of musical greatness. Hailed as the defining voice of his generation, Dylan produces timeless anthems with astonishing frequency. Yet amidst this creative outpouring, a vocal minority accuses the young troubadour of appropriating others' musical work. 

Fast forward to 2004: Producer Brian Burton, better known as Danger Mouse, makes waves by blending The Beatles' iconic "White Album" with Jay-Z's "The Black Album" to create the groundbreaking "Grey Album." This innovative mashup ignites the digital landscape, drawing both widespread acclaim and legal fire from The Beatles' label, which floods the internet with cease-and-desist orders citing "unfair competition and brand dilution." At its core, this controversy revolves around a simple truth: "The Grey Album" represents the art of remixing pushed to bold new frontiers.

A documentary offering an unfiltered glimpse into the life of chart-topping singer George Michael will be featured at this year's Berlin Film Festival. Titled "A Different Story," the film will be screened in the Panorama section of the festival, which takes place from February 10 to 20. The documentary includes candid discussions by Michael covering both his illustrious career and personal journey, from his early years with Wham! to more recent events. George Michael will be present at the festival to introduce the screening on February 16. Accompanying him will be the film's director, Southan Morris, and executive producer, Andy Stephens. The 93-minute film delves into Michael's beginnings with Wham!, his subsequent solo career, his legal disputes with Sony, and his outspoken views on the Iraq War and American politics.

The world's largest and most devastating environmental and industrial project is located in the heart of Canada's boreal forest, the largest and most pristine forest on Earth. This vast, untouched wilderness stretches across northern Canada and is home to the George River caribou herd in Labrador, which is the largest remaining wild caribou herd in the world with approximately 400,000 animals. Sadly, during my visit, I was unable to spot any caribou, though the antlers serve as evidence of their presence. Throughout the boreal forest, we are blessed with an incredible abundance of wetlands. Globally, wetlands are among the most endangered ecosystems. These vital ecosystems play a crucial role in purifying air and water, sequestering significant amounts of greenhouse gases, and providing habitat to a vast diversity of species.

The past weekend was a respite of unbridled indulgence, marked by a long-overdue reunion with the soothing allure of nature and the sonic landscapes of Nevermore, a band whose discography I had inexplicably neglected for far too long. This period of relaxation also afforded me the opportunity to devour a substantial amount of literature, culminating in the completion of three novels: Bruce Sterling's 'Heavy Weather', Larry Niven's 'World of Ptavvs', and Octavia Butler's 'Parable of the Sower'. Of these, 'Heavy Weather' proved to be a captivating cyberpunk escapade, set against the backdrop of a dystopian near-future United States ravaged by the devastating consequences of global warming and societal chaos. While Sterling's masterful storytelling and meticulous world-building rendered the narrative both convincing and engaging, I couldn't help but feel that the experience, though enjoyable, was somewhat lacking in depth and emotional resonance. The characters, though skillfully crafted and vividly realized, failed to leave an indelible mark on my psyche, leaving me to conclude that, although 'Heavy Weather' is undoubtedly a well-crafted novel, it falls short of being truly exceptional.

Khyatt (non-privileged).pst - El Paso Tests Market for Mainline Expansion 

El Paso Natural Gas received a clear message when its last open season for 1.2 billion cubic feet per day (bcf/d) of firm transportation capacity garnered bids totaling 14.4 bcf/d. In response, the company has launched an open season to assess market interest in expanding its mainline and address regulatory concerns regarding the capacity constraints at the California border. El Paso is facing significant pressure from regulators and shippers to expand due to California's immense demand and soaring prices. In a letter dated January 3, Daniel M. Adamson, Director of FERC's Office of Energy Projects, urged El Paso to consider modifying its Line 2000 project to "assist the difficult situation" currently affecting the California gas market.

We examine the relationship between two distinct approaches for constructing the mirror partner of a Calabi-Yau orbifold. This orbifold arises as the quotient of a hypersurface \( x_m \) in weighted projective space—defined by a quasi-homogeneous polynomial \( w_m \)—under the action of an appropriate subgroup \( g \) of its phase symmetries.  

The first method, known as the Berglund-Hübsch-Krawitz (BHK) construction, involves a dual weighted projective space and the quotient of a corresponding hypersurface \( x_{m^t} \) by a dual group \( g^t \). The second approach, Batyrev's construction, derives the mirror partner as a hypersurface in a toric variety associated with the reflexive polytope dual to the original Calabi-Yau orbifold's defining polytope.  

Here, we present a concise demonstration of the equivalence between these two constructions.

In the framework of general relativity, the Kerr spacetime stands as the singular stationary solution for an isolated black hole, characterized by a distinctive multipolar structure that is solely determined by its mass and spin. This work introduces a novel methodology for extracting multipole moments from arbitrary stationary spacetimes, which is subsequently applied to a vast array of horizonless microstate geometries. Notably, these geometries can disrupt the axial and equatorial symmetries inherent to the Kerr metric, thereby exhibiting a far more intricate multipolar structure. This, in turn, offers a unique opportunity to phenomenologically constrain fuzzball models. Our analysis reveals compelling numerical evidence suggesting that the multipole moments of these geometries are generally more pronounced than those of a Kerr black hole with equivalent mass and spin. Presently, measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates impose only modest constraints on fuzzball models. However, forthcoming gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals via the LISA space mission are poised to significantly enhance these bounds, yielding improvements of several orders of magnitude.

Debra Hill, the renowned screenwriter and producer behind the iconic 1978 horror classic "Halloween," has passed away in Los Angeles at the age of 54, succumbing to a long battle with cancer. Hill, who co-wrote the seminal film with John Carpenter, is best known for her groundbreaking work on the groundbreaking horror classic that catapulted Jamie Lee Curtis to stardom as a babysitter terrorized by a psychopathic killer. Directed by Carpenter, the film's phenomenal success, grossing over $60 million worldwide, shattered the record for an independent film at the time, cementing its status as a horror icon. Hill's impressive body of work also includes collaborations with Carpenter on "Escape from New York" and "The Fog." Born in New Jersey, Hill began her illustrious career as a production assistant, working her way up the ranks to become an assistant director and second-unit director before partnering with Carpenter to create some of the most enduring horror classics of the 1970s. Density-based clustering operates on the principle of associating groups with distinct characteristics of the underlying data distribution. By grounding the analysis in an assumed but unobserved population structure, the clustering task can be formalized within a traditional statistical inference framework. Here, the optimal clustering is defined by the true density function’s inherent partitioning.  

A nonparametric implementation of this method, termed *modal clustering*, establishes a direct relationship between clusters and the regions of attraction around density peaks. Practically, this requires estimating the density nonparametrically, where the choice of smoothing—critical in shaping both the density and its modal features—plays a decisive role in determining the final cluster configuration.  

This study examines the problem of density estimation for modal clustering through an asymptotic lens, exploring its theoretical foundations and implications.

A witty observer once remarked that politics is essentially "show business for the less photogenic." By that measure, I suppose I've truly made it. It's particularly humbling to deliver a TED Talk as a politician here in the UK, where our profession's reputation has plummeted to remarkable depths following the expenses scandal. The situation has become so dire that I recently encountered an amusing anecdote: researchers contemplated substituting laboratory rats with politicians for their experiments. When questioned about this unconventional approach, they offered three compelling justifications - an abundant supply of politicians, public indifference toward their fate, and the simple fact that there are certain indignities even rodents would refuse to endure. (Laughter) Since this audience appreciates empirical evidence, I'll begin with a particularly data-intensive visual.

Ireland delivered a commanding performance to overcome Scotland at Murrayfield, keeping their Six Nations Grand Slam aspirations firmly alive. Despite an early 8-0 lead for the Scots, courtesy of Hugo Southwell's try, Ireland seized control before halftime with tries from locks Malcolm O'Kelly and Paul O'Connell. The visitors further extended their dominance after the break, as winger Denis Hickie crossed for a third try while Ronan O'Gara contributed 13 points with his boot. Though Jon Petrie touched down for Scotland's second try, late scores from John Hayes and Gavin Duffy ensured Ireland's triumph.  

With two hard-fought away victories secured, Eddie O'Sullivan's squad now turns its attention to hosting England at Lansdowne Road in two weeks' time. Meanwhile, Scotland, still searching for their first Six Nations win under coach Matt Williams, face another challenge when Italy visit Edinburgh—their inability to convert pressure into points remaining a persistent concern.

"While even the most reviled creatures in nature possess secrets of great importance, it's unlikely that anyone would welcome an approaching swarm of cockroaches. Robust systems are capable of maintaining stability in the face of complex and uncharted environments. Notably, cockroaches have evolved the remarkable ability to self-stabilize when navigating rough terrain. When equipped with a jet pack or subjected to a perturbation such as an earthquake, we found that their exquisitely tuned legs enable them to self-stabilize without requiring any cognitive input. Autonomous vehicle (AV) control has been shown to significantly reduce the impact of selfish routing on network efficiency. However, the assumption that AVs would readily allow themselves to be controlled externally by a central authority is unrealistic. In this paper, we propose a novel joint routing and pricing control scheme that aims to incentivize AVs to seek centrally controlled system-optimal routing by minimizing tolls while ensuring user equilibrium and human-driven vehicles are subject to a congestion charge. The problem is formulated as a bilevel optimization problem, where dynamic tolls are optimized in the upper level, whereas the lower level is a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model considering a mixed fleet of AVs and human-driven vehicles.

When you see me, what comes to mind? Perhaps you see a woman of faith, an expert, or maybe even someone akin to a sister. On the other hand, you might perceive me as oppressed, brainwashed, or even mistakenly view me as a threat or a mere delay in an airport security line—which, humorously enough, can be quite accurate at times. (laughter) If some of your perceptions tend toward the negative, I can hardly blame you. These misconceptions largely stem from the media's portrayal of individuals who look like me. In fact, a study revealed that 80 percent of the news coverage related to Islam and Muslims is negative. Additionally, research indicates that most Americans claim not to personally know a Muslim. Perhaps this means they don’t engage much with their Uber drivers. (laughter) For those who have yet to meet a Muslim, I’m delighted to make your acquaintance.

Spirit awards hail sideways the comedy sideways has dominated this year's independent spirit awards, winning all six of the awards for which it was nominated. It was named best film while alexander payne won best director and best screenplay, along with writing partner jim taylor. It also won acting awards for stars paul giamatti, thomas haden church and virginia madsen. Sideways is tipped to do well at sunday's oscars, with five nominations. The awards, now in their 20th year, are given to films made outside the traditional studio system, and are traditionally held the day before the oscars. Other winners included catalina sandino moreno, who took best actress for her role as a drug smuggler in the colombian drama maria full of grace. Moreno is also nominated for best actress at the oscars. The best first screenplay award went to joshua marston for maria full of grace.

Routing controllability of autonomous vehicles (avs) has been shown to reduce the impact of selfish routing on network efficiency. However, the assumption that avs would readily allow themselves to be controlled externally by a central agency is unrealistic. In this paper, we propose a joint routing and pricing control scheme that aims to incentivize avs to seek centrally controlled system optimal (so) routing by saving on tolls while user equilibrium (ue) seeking avs and human-driven vehicles (hvs) are subject to a congestion charge. The problem is formulated as a bi-level optimization, in which dynamic tolls are optimized in the upper level, whereas the lower level is a mixed equilibrium simulation-based dynamic traffic assignment model considering mixed fleet of avs and hvs.

This paper presents a millimeter-scale cmos 64$\times$64 single charged particle radiation detector system for external beam cancer radiotherapy. A 1$\times$1 $\mu m^2$ diode measures energy deposition by a single charged particle in the depletion region, and the array design provides a large detection area of 512$\times$512 $\mu m^2$. Instead of sensing the voltage drop caused by radiation, the proposed system measures the pulse width, i.e., the time it takes for the voltage to return to its baseline. This obviates the need for using power-hungry and large analog-to-digital converters. A prototype asic is fabricated in tsmc 65 nm lp cmos process and consumes the average static power of 0.535 mw under 1.2 v analog and digital power supply. The functionality of the whole system is successfully verified in a clinical 67.5 mev proton beam setting.

This study explores multi-agent discrete-event systems operating under partial observation. The system's agents are organized into distinct groups, where members within each group exhibit isomorphic state transition structures. This structural similarity enables the representation of all agents within a group through a single standardized template. Leveraging this template-based approach, we develop a scalable supervisory control framework that maintains constant computational complexity and state space requirements, irrespective of the number of agents. Notably, the proposed supervisor remains valid when agents are dynamically added or removed from the system, eliminating the need for recomputation. We extend previous work by establishing sufficient conditions that guarantee both safety and maximal permissiveness for the template-level supervisor under partial observation. A practical example demonstrates the effectiveness of our scalable supervisory synthesis methodology.

The estimation of the intrinsic dimension of a dataset is a crucial step in most dimensionality reduction techniques. This article introduces intrinsic, an R package that offers state-of-the-art likelihood-based estimators for the intrinsic dimension of a dataset. The package includes novel methods, specifically the two-nn, gride, and hidalgo models, which are designed to be easily accessible through a set of high-level, user-friendly functions that leverage efficient, low-level routines. Intrinsic dimension estimation encompasses two categories  homogeneous and heterogeneous models. The package provides functions for both homogeneous and heterogeneous methods, including the two-nn and gride models, which can be applied under both frequentist and Bayesian frameworks. Additionally, it features hidalgo, a Bayesian mixture model, with an efficient Gibbs sampler implementation.

Iraq to invite phone licence bids iraq is to invite bids for two telephone licences, saying it wants to significantly boost nationwide coverage over the next decade. Bids have been invited from local, arab and foreign companies, iraq's ministry of communications said. The winner will work in partnership with the iraqi telecommunications and post company (itpc). The firms will install and operate a fixed phone network, providing voice, fax and internet services. The ministry said that it wanted to increase iraq's "very low telephone service penetration rate from about 4.5% today to about 25% within 10 years." it also hopes to develop a "highly visible and changeable telecommunication sector". Details of the bidding and tender process will be published on the ministry's website on 9 february. It also is planning a road-show for investors in amman, jordan.

Low-symmetry 2D materials, including resorbed and reshifted monolayers, black phosphorus, group-IV monochalcogenides, and borophene, exhibit more intricate atomic arrangements than the hexagonal lattices of graphene, hexagonal boron nitride, and transition metal dichalcogenides. The reduced symmetries of these emerging materials lead to a diverse range of electronic, optical, valley, and spin responses, including ferroelasticity, ferroelectricity, magnetism, spin-wave phenomena, large nonlinear optical properties, photogalvanic effects, and superconductivity. Additionally, these materials can exhibit novel electronic topological properties, nonlinear elastic properties, and structural phase transformations due to their low symmetry. The "Beyond Graphene: Low-Symmetry and Anisotropic 2D Materials" special topic brings together recent experimental and theoretical research on these innovative materials.

In the framework of general relativity, the distinctive stationary solution for an isolated black hole is the Kerr spacetime, characterized by a unique multipolar structure dependent solely on its mass and spin. We develop a comprehensive method to extract the multipole moments of arbitrary stationary spacetimes and apply it to a vast family of horizonless microstate geometries. These geometries can break the axial and equatorial symmetry of the Kerr metric, exhibiting a much richer multipolar structure that offers a gateway to constrain fuzzball models phenomenologically. Our findings reveal numerical evidence indicating that all multipole moments are typically significantly larger (in absolute value) than those of a Kerr black hole with the same mass and spin. Current measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates impose only modest constraints on fuzzballs, whereas future gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals with the space-based mission LISA will significantly enhance these bounds by several orders of magnitude.

We study the phenomenology of a hypercharge-zero su(2) triplet scalar whose existence is motivated by two-step electroweak symmetry-breaking. We consider both the possibility that the triplets are stable and contribute to the dark matter density, or that they decay via mixing with the standard model higgs boson. The former is constrained by disappearing charged track searches at the lhc and by dark matter direct detection experiments, while the latter is constrained by existing multilepton collider searches. We find that a two-step electroweak phase transition involving a stable triplet with a negative quadratic term is ruled out by direct detection searches, while an unstable triplet with a mass less than $230\ \mathrm{gev}$ is excluded at $95\%$ confidence level.

In 1903, the 26th President of the United States, Theodore Roosevelt, embarked on a three-day camping excursion in California's breathtaking Yosemite Valley. Roosevelt slept among the towering sequoia groves, braved a snowstorm, and spent countless hours engaging in spirited conversations around the campfire with his host and guide, the renowned conservationist John Muir. Roosevelt, an ardent lover of nature, was invited by Muir for more than just a camping trip - Yosemite was in peril. Although the valley was protected land since 1864, it remained vulnerable to overdevelopment in 1903, at the center of a decades-old struggle to strike a balance between preservation and public use. The contentious battle over Yosemite began in 1849 with the Gold Rush, as miners flocked west in search of gold in the Sierra Nevada foothills.

Tonight, I watched *Troy* with my family, but the film failed to leave a lasting impression. While it certainly had potential, the production seemed overly focused on its grandiose set design—though visually stunning—at the expense of a compelling narrative and cohesive themes. The retelling of the story lacked depth, leaving me emotionally uninvested and ultimately unfulfilled. Character development was notably underwhelming, further diminishing the film's impact. The score, unfortunately, felt derivative, echoing *Gladiator*'s haunting vocals without achieving the same emotional resonance. In *Gladiator*, the mournful backdrop enhanced an already gripping tale, but here, it merely highlighted the film's narrative shortcomings.

The pursuit of causal understanding through observational data lies at the heart of research in health and social sciences. While traditional statistical approaches have sometimes been skeptical of causal interpretations, the emergence of data science presents a transformative moment to reshape analytical paradigms—making causal inference from observational studies an integral component. Building on established frameworks, we categorize data science's scientific objectives into three fundamental domains: descriptive analysis, predictive modeling, and counterfactual reasoning (encompassing causal inference). This systematic classification enables clearer discourse about the distinct data requirements, theoretical foundations, and methodological approaches needed for each analytical purpose. Importantly, we highlight how neglecting to properly articulate the critical role of domain expertise in data interpretation has led to pervasive misconceptions about the practice of data science.

British stars denied major oscars british hopes of winning major oscars were dashed as uk stars failed to win acting and directing prizes. Despite three nominations, mike leigh's vera drake failed to take the director or screenplay awards and there was no prize for star imelda staunton. Kate winslet also lost to best actress hilary swank, while clive owen and sophie okonedo failed to win best supporting actor awards. The uk's andrea arnold won the short film award for her gritty drama wasp. Arnold, who presented children's television shows number 73 and motormouth in the 1980s, said it was "totally overwhelming" to win. Wasp tells the story of a single mother living on the breadline, beating films by fellow uk nominees gary mckendry and ashvin kumar.

A comprehensive study on the thermally induced transverse vibrations of a photonic crystal waveguide (PCW) is presented, shedding light on the intricate dynamics of this complex system. The PCW under investigation comprises two parallel nanobeams separated by a 240 nm vacuum gap, carefully designed to facilitate near-field atom trapping. To elucidate the interplay between the mechanical motion of the beams and the optical properties of the waveguide, sophisticated models have been developed and validated. These models accurately capture the transduction of beam motion into phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe, propagating in a guided mode of the PCW, across a wide range of probe frequencies, including those near and far from the dielectric band edge. This research provides a crucial foundation for assessing the potential impact of thermal fluctuations on the stability of optical atomic traps in close proximity to PCW surfaces. Ultimately, this work aims to establish a robust framework for harnessing the quantum-level interactions between individual phonons, photons, and atoms, thereby paving the way for the realization of quantum optomechanics in this system.

This paper introduces an innovative millimeter-scale CMOS 64x64 radiation detector system designed for detecting single charged particles in external beam cancer radiotherapy. The system features a 1x1 μm² diode that captures the energy deposited by a single charged particle within the depletion region. Its array layout provides an extensive detection area of 512x512 μm². Unlike traditional methods that detect voltage drops from radiation, this system measures the pulse width, which is the time required for the voltage to return to its initial state. This approach eliminates the necessity for large, energy-intensive analog-to-digital converters. A prototype ASIC was manufactured using the TSMC 65 nm LP CMOS process, with an average static power consumption of just 0.535 mW under a 1.2 V analog and digital power supply. The entire system's functionality has been successfully validated in a clinical setting using a 67.5 MeV proton beam.

Deadline nears for fiat-gm deal fiat and general motors (gm) have until midnight on 1 february to settle a disagreement over a potential takeover. The deadline marks the point at which fiat will gain the right to sell its car division to gm, part of an alliance agreed in 2000. Gm, whose own european operations are losing money, no longer wants to own the unprofitable fiat unit. Reports of deadlocked talks sent fiat shares down 1.2% on tuesday, after monday's 4% gain on hopes of a payoff. The us firm is thought to be offering about $2bn (£1.06bn) to extricate itself from the arrangement. It has argued the deal was voided by fiat's decision to sell off fiat's finance arm and halve gm's stake via a capital-raising effort. The 2000 deal resulted from a race between gm and daimlerchrysler to ally with fiat.

Hearts 2-1 livingston hearts wrapped up their scottish cup quarter-final tie against livingston with two goals in the first 10 minutes. Lee miller scored inside the opening 60 seconds, heading over colin meldrum and into the net from a jamie mcallister free kick. Mcallister himself scored the second when he met saul mikoliunas' cross at the back post to put the game beyond the visitors. Craig easton headed in a controversial goal after an hour but hearts held on. The opening goal came after gus bahoken had clumsily fouled miller 35 yards from goal. Mcallister floated the free kick into the livi box and miller outjumped the static visiting defence to loop his header from 10 yards over meldrum. With just 10 minutes on the clock, the home side doubled their lead. Mikoliunas sprinted down the right, crossed to the back post where mcallister raced in to head past meldrum.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (emris) can be classified as dry emris and wet emris based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or the "loss-cone") emris, previsouly considered as the main emri sources for the laser interferometer space antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this letter, we highlight an alternative emri formation channel: (wet) emri formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (mbhs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are intially moving on inclined orbits, and subsequently drives them to migrate towards the mbh - this process boosts the formation rate of emris in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

A poignant documentary offering a deeply personal glimpse into the life of the late music icon George Michael is set to premiere at the prestigious Berlin Film Festival. Titled 'A Different Story', the film will be showcased in the festival's Panorama section, running from 10-20 February. This intimate portrait delves into the highs and lows of Michael's remarkable career, from his early days as a member of the chart-topping duo Wham! to his later years as a solo artist, marked by both creative triumphs and personal struggles. The film features Michael himself, sharing candid insights into his life, including his high-profile battles with the music industry, his outspoken stance against the Iraq War, and his critiques of American politics. To mark the occasion, George Michael will attend the festival on 16 February to introduce the screening, joined by the film's director, Southan Morris, and executive producer, Andy Stephens. Clocking in at 93 minutes, 'A Different Story' promises to be a powerful and moving tribute to the enduring legacy of a music legend.

Notification of Disruption to PX Day-Ahead Auction Market

To: All PX Market Participants
From: John Yurkanin, Chief Operating Officer
Date: Monday, December 11, 2000

Subject: Urgent: Loss of Continuity in PX Day-Ahead Auction due to ISO Rule Changes

We are writing to inform you that the recent emergency tariff filing by the California ISO has significantly impacted the PX Day-Ahead Auction market. The introduction of a hybrid pricing system, combining soft and hard caps, has created an unacceptable level of risk for all market participants. This new system is unlikely to achieve the ISO's desired reduction in real-time energy sales and procurement, as suppliers will continue to prioritize the higher price opportunities offered by the ISO.

In light of this development, the PX is taking immediate action to address the issue. We are petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to permit adjustment bids on any submitted schedule at any relevant price, in order to restore continuity and stability to the market.

The Bloggies, a prestigious annual award, has officially commenced its voting process to honor the most exceptional weblogs of the year. These online platforms serve as personal outlets for individuals to express their thoughts and opinions. Following the announcement of nominations on Sunday, the official website experienced an overwhelming surge in traffic, resulting in a temporary shutdown due to the sheer volume of visitors. This year's awards feature 30 distinct categories, ranging from the top regional weblog to the best-kept-secret weblog, showcasing the diversity and creativity of the blogging community. The past year has been a remarkable one for weblogs, with a prominent US dictionary recognizing "blog" as the word of 2004. According to Technorati, a leading blog search engine, there are approximately six million active weblogs, with over 12,000 new additions daily. A study by the Pew Internet and American Life research think-tank reveals that a new weblog is created every 5.8 seconds. However, despite this rapid growth, less than 40% of existing weblogs are updated at least once every two months, highlighting the challenges of maintaining a consistent online presence.

I initially purchased this game during its early development phase, back when the in-game economy relied solely on spacebux and before the introduction of a tech tree. Those were the game's golden days. Every weapon was equally formidable, creating a perfectly balanced chaos where any player could defeat another at any moment. The game also generously provided unlimited weapon slots and five customizable loadouts—a far cry from the current restrictive system. For a title that boasts "bajillions of guns," it's disappointing that players are limited to just six weapon slots and three loadouts before being forced to pay for additional capacity. At my peak, I had collected enough weapons to fill three full pages and was actively brainstorming new armament concepts—that creative freedom was what made the experience truly enjoyable for me.

The past week has been a whirlwind, and I can hardly believe it's already come to a close. Yesterday, I had the opportunity to drive by our new home, and I was thrilled to see the front window filled with boxes - a tangible sign that our move is finally within reach. With closing potentially just around the corner, I'm growing increasingly excited at the prospect of receiving the keys. If all goes according to plan this week, we're on track to finalize the sale a week from Wednesday, and I can barely contain my anticipation. Last night was a late one, as Bethany and I indulged in a movie marathon that stretched well into the early hours of the morning. It's been a cinematic week, to say the least, and I thought I'd take a moment to share my thoughts on the films we've been watching. First up was "Thirteen," a movie that I firmly believe every parent of a teenager should see. I was pleasantly surprised by its thought-provoking portrayal of adolescence, and I found it to be a far more impactful film than I had initially expected. In stark contrast, our second feature of the night was "Gigli," a movie that has been widely panned by critics. While it's safe to say that the film lived up to its notorious reputation, we felt compelled to see it for ourselves, if only to satisfy our curiosity.

Within general relativity, the unique stationary solution of an isolated black hole is the kerr spacetime, which has a peculiar multipolar structure depending only on its mass and spin. We develop a general method to extract the multipole moments of arbitrary stationary spacetimes and apply it to a large family of horizonless microstate geometries. The latter can break the axial and equatorial symmetry of the kerr metric and have a much richer multipolar structure, which provides a portal to constrain fuzzball models phenomenologically. We find numerical evidence that all multipole moments are typically larger (in absolute value) than those of a kerr black hole with the same mass and spin. Current measurements of the quadrupole moment of black-hole candidates could place only mild constraints on fuzzballs, while future gravitational-wave detections of extreme mass-ratio inspirals with the space mission lisa will improve these bounds by orders of magnitude.

Roxy music on isle of wight bill roxy music will appear at june's isle of wight music festival, along with morrissey, supergrass and idlewild. Rem have already been confirmed as headliners for the three-day event, which takes place from 10-12 june. Original band members bryan ferry, phil manzerana, andy mackay and paul thompson will take to the stage on the saturday night of the festival. The band are also working in the studio on new material, their first since the 1982 album avalon. Roxy music were formed in 1971 by ferry, mackay and brian eno, with manzanera joining the following year. Their first hit was 1972's virginia plain, with other hits including street life, love is the drug and the 1981 john lennon cover, jealous guy.

In the summer of 1903, a pivotal moment in American conservation history unfolded in California's breathtaking Yosemite Valley, as President Theodore Roosevelt embarked on a three-day camping excursion. Under the majestic canopy of towering sequoia trees, Roosevelt immersed himself in the unspoiled beauty of nature, braving a snowstorm and engaging in profound conversations with his host, the renowned conservationist John Muir, around the warm glow of the campfire. While Roosevelt's affinity for the outdoors was well-documented, Muir's invitation extended far beyond a leisurely camping trip – it was a call to action, as Yosemite teetered on the brink of destruction. Despite being designated as protected land in 1864, the valley remained vulnerable to the threats of overdevelopment in 1903, caught in the midst of a decades-long struggle to strike a delicate balance between preservation and public access – a challenge that proved far more daunting than it seemed. The seeds of this contentious battle were sown during the 1849 gold rush, when a surge of prospectors flocked to the Sierra Nevada foothills, setting in motion a chain of events that would ultimately determine the fate of Yosemite.

Jdasovic.nsf the spreadsheet below gives the implicit commodity based on the effective date of four different rates for the same rate schedule (schedule dr). The rate schedule effective august 2000 through september 2000 yields a much larger commodity charge because sdg&e customers will receive a lump sum reimbursement from sdg&e over those two months. Sdg&e customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average for june 2000 through january 2001 is 6.2 cts and the implicit commodity average from february 2001 through december 2001 is 7.3 cts. The average implicit commodity from june 2000 through december 2001 is 6.9 cts for deliveries starting september 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cts.

This study explores the realm of multi-agent discrete-event systems, where agents operate under partial observation and can be categorized into distinct groups based on their isomorphic state transition structures. By leveraging this template-based approach, a novel, scalable supervisory framework is developed, which decouples the state size and computational complexity from the number of agents involved. Notably, this framework remains invariant to changes in the agent population, eliminating the need for recalibration when agents are added or removed. Building upon previous research, this work extends the existing results to accommodate partial observation, introducing necessary conditions for ensuring safety and maximal permissiveness in the scalable, least restrictive supervisor. A comprehensive example is provided to demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed supervisory synthesis methodology.

Spintronic nanodevices exhibit ultrafast nonlinear dynamics and recurrence behaviors on a nanosecond scale, paving the way for advancements in spintronic reservoir computing (RC) systems. This study introduces and models two physical RC systems: one utilizing a single magnetic skyrmion memristor (MSM) and the other comprising 24 spin-torque nano-oscillators (STNOs). These systems were designed to tackle image classification tasks and nonlinear dynamic system predictions, respectively. Our micromagnetic simulations highlight the nonlinear responses of MSM and STNOs when stimulated by current pulses, demonstrating that an RC system based on a single MSM excels in image classification tasks, such as recognizing handwritten digits. Furthermore, a physical RC system composed of 24 STNOs effectively addresses complex and previously unknown nonlinear dynamic problems, as evidenced in tasks involving a second-order nonlinear dynamic system and NARMA10.

Mutation-based fuzzing typically relies on an initial set of non-crashing seed inputs (a corpus) from which to generate new inputs through mutation. However, this approach can lead to inefficiencies due to the lack of diversity in the corpus, resulting in wasted fuzzing effort through exhaustive mutation of all available seeds. To address this, fuzzers often employ distillation tools, such as afl-cmin, to select the smallest subset of seeds that triggers the same range of instrumentation data points as the full corpus. By minimizing the number and cumulative size of the seeds, fuzzing can become more efficient in finding bugs in real-world software. In this work, we present the results of 34+ CPU-years of fuzzing with five distillation approaches to systematically explore their impact on bug discovery.

You can't help it, you can't evade it: a piece of you will always live in me. Even if I never carried you child, even if I was never married to you, even if I was never an official pair to you... I loved you immensely, and to this day the feeling carries an echo through my heart. Forever will I always be reminded of the very few nights we spent together, hearts and limbs entwined in a soul-embrace, drenched in the salty darkness of an evening by the beach... Or a kiss under the rain. Whenever I get the feeling of a dark glass puncturing my heart, you will be there. That piece of you... Bleeding the eternal love that once lived in me... And still remains. Forever.

Processing spontaneous conversations in real-world environments, like those found in child-centered recordings, is recognized as one of the most complex audio challenges. Successfully developing speech processing models capable of handling such a diverse range of conditions would greatly benefit language acquisition research, which focuses on the quantity and quality of speech children are exposed to and produce. Additionally, these models would be invaluable for early diagnosis and evaluating the impact of interventions. In this paper, we introduce our methodology for creating an open-source neural network designed to categorize audio segments into vocalizations by the child wearing the recording device, vocalizations from other children, adult male speech, and adult female speech. To achieve this, we compiled a diverse set of child-centered corpora, amassing a total of 260 hours of recordings spanning 10 languages.

Podcasts mark rise of diy radio an apple ipod or other digital music players can hold anything up to 10,000 songs, which is a lot of space to fill. But more and more ipod owners are filling that space with audio content created by an unpredictable assortment of producers. It is called "podcasting" and its strongest proponent is former mtv host and vj (video jockey) adam curry. Podcasting takes its name from the apple ipod, although you do not need an ipod to create one or to listen to a podcast. A podcast is basically an internet-based radio show which podcasters create, usually in the comfort of their own home. They need only a microphone, a pc, and some editing software. They then upload their shows to the internet and others can download and listen to them, all for free.

Determining the intrinsic dimensionality of data is a critical preliminary step in dimensionality reduction. This paper presents *intrinsic*, an R package that introduces cutting-edge likelihood-based estimators for assessing dataset dimensionality. Specifically, the package implements three advanced methodologies: the Two-NN, Gride, and HIDALGO models. Designed for accessibility, *intrinsic* offers user-friendly high-level functions supported by optimized computational routines.  

The package features two distinct classes of dimensionality estimators: homogeneous and heterogeneous. The homogeneous category includes the Two-NN and Gride models, which support both frequentist and Bayesian inference. The heterogeneous category comprises HIDALGO, a Bayesian mixture model, accompanied by an efficient Gibbs sampling implementation for robust analysis.

Ranking algorithms are a crucial component of online platforms, including search engines and recommender systems. Our research reveals a counterintuitive phenomenon where the fewer items that report a given signal, the higher the collective traffic they receive. This few-get-richer effect arises in scenarios where there are limited categories of items, such as left-leaning or right-leaning news sources, and ranking is based on popularity. We prove analytically that this effect emerges when people tend to click on top-ranked items and have diverse preferences among item categories. Using simulations, we explore the impact of various factors on the strength of the effect, including the number of categories and user preferences. To validate our findings, we conduct an online experiment with human participants, providing valuable insights into the dissemination of misinformation.

Over the weekend, Governor Davis and Senator Polanco reached an informal agreement in which Senator Polanco agreed to sponsor the SoCal MOU bill. Polanco, a Democrat, is currently embroiled in a personal scandal that has reportedly left his political career in jeopardy. Instead of seeking another term in the Senate, Polanco plans to run for Mayor of Los Angeles. The bill is anticipated to be introduced during the new extraordinary session, starting today or tomorrow. Initially, it will be reviewed by the Senate Energy Committee. Halloween writer debra hill dies screenwriter and producer debra hill, best known for her work on the 70s horror classic halloween, has died in los angeles aged 54. Hill, who had been suffering from cancer, co-wrote the 1978 film, which starred jamie lee curtis as a babysitter terrorised by a psychopath. Directed by john carpenter, it made over $60m (£31.3m) worldwide - a record for independent film at that time. Hill also worked with carpenter on escape from new york and the fog. Born in new jersey, hill began her career as a production assistant and worked her way through the ranks, becoming an assistant director and second-unit director before she began collaborating with carpenter. She was regarded by many as a pioneering woman in film, taking on jobs in the 70s that were more commonly taken by men.

Weight pruning is universally recognized as a simple yet powerful technique for reducing redundancy in deep neural networks (DNNs), enabling accelerated performance across diverse hardware platforms. However, conventional pruning methods typically compromise between model precision and structural regularity, often resulting in diminished inference accuracy and suboptimal on-device acceleration.  

To address these limitations, we present a novel sparsity paradigm—pattern-based sparsity—which integrates both pattern and connectivity sparsity. This approach not only preserves high accuracy but also ensures hardware efficiency. Through meticulously crafted sparsity patterns, our method achieves unprecedented improvements in both model precision and feature extraction across varied DNN architectures and datasets.  

Furthermore, our pattern-aware pruning framework seamlessly combines pattern library extraction, optimal pattern selection, simultaneous pattern and connectivity pruning, and weight training. By leveraging this innovative pattern-based sparsity, our solution inherently aligns with compiler optimizations, enabling highly efficient DNN execution on mobile devices.

However, in 2003, Khodorkovsky was arrested on charges of fraud and embezzlement, and the company was nationalized by the Russian government. Since then, Yukos has been in a state of legal limbo, with the company's assets frozen and its operations suspended.

You’ve been accused of a crime you did not commit. It’s impossible to prove your innocence. If you insist that you’re innocent anyway, you’ll likely be found guilty and executed. But if you confess, apologize, and implicate others for good measure, you’ll go free. Do you give a false confession— or risk a public hanging?this was the choice facing those accused of witchcraft in the village of salem, massachusetts between february 1692 and may 1693. They were the victims of paranoia about the supernatural, misdirected religious fervor— and a justice system that valued repentance over truth.salem was settled in 1626 by puritans, a group of english protestants. Life was strict and isolated for the people of salem. Battles with their native american neighbors and groups of french settlers were commonplace.

Extreme mass ratio inspirals (EMRIs) can be categorized as dry EMRIs and wet EMRIs based on their formation mechanisms. Dry (or the 'loss-cone') EMRIs, previously considered as the primary EMRI sources for the laser interferometer space antenna, are primarily produced by multi-body scattering in the nuclear star cluster and gravitational capture. In this paper, we highlight an alternative EMRI formation channel  (wet) EMRI formation assisted by the accretion flow around accreting galactic-center massive black holes (MBHs). In this channel, the accretion disk captures stellar-mass black holes that are initially moving on inclined orbits, and subsequently drives them to migrate towards the MBH - this process boosts the formation rate of EMRIs in such galaxies by orders of magnitude.

Deep learning has solved many problems that are out of reach of heuristic algorithms. It has also been successfully applied in wireless communications, even though the current radio systems are well-understood and optimal algorithms exist for many tasks. While some gains have been obtained by learning individual parts of a receiver, a better approach is to jointly learn the whole receiver. This, however, often results in a challenging nonlinear problem, for which the optimal solution is infeasible to implement. To this end, we propose a deep fully convolutional neural network, deeprx, which executes the whole receiver pipeline from frequency domain signal stream to uncoded bits in a 5g-compliant fashion. We facilitate accurate channel estimation by constructing the input of the convolutional neural network in a very specific manner using both the data and pilot symbols.

Twelve years ago, I began exploring a pressing issue in the context of developing countries. While reflecting on this topic, I realized that it's crucial to consider the global perspective. If you visualize a world map, you'll notice that every country has areas where good teachers are in short supply. These are often the very places where they are needed most urgently. This is an ironic challenge - good teachers are hesitant to work in the regions where they are most needed. In 1999, I embarked on a mission to address this problem with a pioneering experiment in New Delhi. My goal was to identify a simple yet effective solution to this complex issue.

Sustainability concerns itself with the specifics of what is caught, where it is caught, and how it is caught. However, I am more interested in understanding the people involved—their motivations and their stories. I care about the individuals who contribute to my dining experience and how my choices affect their lives and vice versa. I want to learn why they fish and how they depend on the ocean's resources for their livelihoods. By gaining this understanding, we can transform our view of seafood from just a consumable product to an opportunity to rejuvenate our ecosystems. It allows us to appreciate the seafood we are lucky enough to consume. So, what do we call this approach? I believe it is best described as "restorative seafood." While sustainability focuses on enduring and maintaining, restoration emphasizes replenishing and advancing.

Warframe initially provides an entertaining experience that lasts for the first few hours of gameplay. However, after this initial excitement, players may notice a repetitive pattern in its combat, enemies, and overall gameplay. One significant advantage of Warframe is the dedication of its developers, who frequently release updates that introduce substantial content, including sweeping system changes, new Warframes, and additional features and tweaks. While Warframe is enjoyable for casual play, it may not maintain your interest for more than a few months unless you genuinely enjoy grinding. The silver lining is that the game is free, making it worthwhile to give it a try. If you're seeking a quick gaming experience, it's definitely worth exploring.

Where does all the carbon we release go? carbon works in a natural cycle, present in all living things: in the soil, in the oceans, in the atmosphere and in rocks deep underground. When plants and other living things die, much of their stored carbon makes its way back to the atmosphere until new plants grow and reabsorb the carbon.but over millions of years, some of the carbon stored in ancient trees and sea life becomes rock and some becomes fossil fuels: coal, oil and natural gas. Natural events like volcanic eruptions release some of the carbon trapped in rock. And human activities, like burning fossil fuels, also release some of that prehistoric carbon.today, humans release around 60 times more carbon than all the volcanoes on the planet each year.

Good afternoon, esteemed audience at TEDxKRAKOW, I am thrilled to be speaking with you today about a phenomenon that is revolutionizing the world and has the power to shape our collective future. I'd like to begin with a thought-provoking anecdote, reminiscent of a Monty Python-esque sketch. Imagine it's December 15, 2010. You're presented with an extraordinary opportunity  a crystal ball that can reveal the future with unwavering accuracy. The catch is that you must share this vision with the world. Your curiosity gets the better of you, and you take the bet. One hour later, you find yourself on the set of a national television show, about to share your extraordinary experience with a captivated audience. You look into the crystal ball, and the future unfolds before your eyes.

Have you ever experienced a moment when you start reflecting on life? Like when you're in math class, utterly bored, and you begin to wonder what processes in your brain enable you to understand math. You become aware of your own existence and question why you're there in that moment. It dawns on you how extraordinary it is just to be alive. Then, suddenly, you realize how ridiculous it is to be caught up in such a random train of thought. Has that ever happened to you? I really dislike it when it does. You know what else I can't stand? Block schedules. Who came up with that concept? It's obvious that sitting through a two-and-a-half-hour class on any subject is nothing short of cruel and unusual punishment.

A woman from the United States has filed a lawsuit against Hewlett-Packard (HP), alleging that its printer ink cartridges are designed to expire on a specific date. This woman, hailing from Georgia and choosing not to reveal her identity, claims that a chip inside these cartridges signals the printer to require a refill even when it's not necessary. The lawsuit aims to include all individuals in the United States who have bought an HP inkjet printer since February 2001. HP, known as the largest printer manufacturer globally, has not provided any comments on this legal action. It is known that HP ink cartridges use chip technology to detect low ink levels and alert users to replace them. However, the lawsuit contends that these chips also deactivate the cartridges after a set period, regardless of whether or not they are actually empty.

For the first time, digital music downloads have been integrated into the prestigious US Singles Chart, marking a significant evolution in how Billboard's Hot 100 rankings are calculated. This groundbreaking change combines download sales—previously tracked separately—with traditional physical single sales and radio airplay data to create a more comprehensive measure of a song's popularity.  

Currently, Green Day's *Boulevard of Broken Dreams* holds the No. 2 spot on Billboard's Pop Chart while leading the Digital Chart. A similar shift is expected in the UK Singles Chart later this year, following the US model.  

Billboard has also introduced a second new ranking, the Pop 100, which merges digital and physical sales with airplay data—though it exclusively considers radio stations that feature chart-topping tracks. This dual expansion reflects the growing influence of digital music in shaping industry trends.

Nonlinear fluorescence emission has become a valuable tool for achieving high spatial resolution in optical imaging. In this study, we explored the fluorescence anomalous saturating effect associated with nitrogen vacancy defects in diamond. When exposed to high-power laser excitation, we observed a reduction in fluorescence that increased the nonlinearity of the emission. This change altered the spatial frequency distribution of the resulting fluorescence image. To capture detailed high spatial frequency information, we employed a differential excitation protocol, which involved modulating the power of the excitation laser. This approach enhanced the spatial resolution of imaging by approximately 1.6 times compared to conventional confocal microscopy. Owing to the straightforward nature of both the experimental setup and data processing, we anticipate that this technique will be highly beneficial for increasing the spatial resolution in applications such as sensing and biological labeling using defects in solids.

**The Essence of Restorative Seafood**  

Sustainability defines the methods, locations, and species involved in fishing—but for me, the deeper meaning lies in the *who* and the *why*. I seek to understand the individuals behind the seafood on my plate—their stories, their livelihoods, and the ways our choices shape each other’s lives. Why do they fish? How does the ocean sustain them?  

This awareness transforms seafood from a mere commodity into a powerful tool for ecological renewal. It invites us to honor the food we consume while actively contributing to the revival of our oceans.  

So what do we name this vision? *Restorative seafood*. While sustainability focuses on preservation, restoration goes further—it’s about regeneration, growth, and leaving the waters richer than we found them.

Subject: Urgent: Impact of ISO Rule Changes on PX Day Ahead Auction

To: All PX Market Participants  
From: John Yurkanin, Chief Operating Officer  
Date: Monday, December 11, 2000

I am writing to address a critical issue affecting the PX Day Ahead Auction caused by recent rule changes implemented by the California ISO. The emergency tariff filing submitted last Friday has disrupted the continuity of our market operations. The introduction of both soft and hard caps has created a significant disconnect, posing unacceptable risks for all participants. Contrary to the ISO's intentions of decreasing real-time energy sales and procurement, this approach is likely to drive suppliers towards the more lucrative pricing opportunities offered by the ISO. In response, the PX is actively petitioning Terry Winter of the ISO to permit immediate submission of adjustment bids at any relevant price for already submitted schedules. We believe this is necessary to restore stability and ensure competitive market dynamics.

Density-based clustering relies on the concept of linking groups to specific features of the probability distribution underlying the data, where the true, yet unknown, population structure serves as a reference. This approach frames the clustering problem in a standard inferential setting, where the concept of ideal population clustering is defined as the partition induced by the true density function. The nonparametric formulation of this approach, known as modal clustering, establishes a correspondence between the groups and the domains of attraction of the density modes. Operationally, a nonparametric density estimate is required, and the proper selection of the amount of smoothing, which governs the shape of the density and potentially the modal structure, is crucial to identify the final partition. In this work, we address the issue of density estimation for modal clustering from an asymptotic perspective, exploring the theoretical foundations and practical implications of this approach.

This weekend, the AAA's Championships in Sheffield will play host to eight of Ireland's top athletes vying for a spot in the European Indoor Championships, including Ballymena sprinter Paul Brizzel. Notably absent from the competition will be US-based Alistair Cragg and Mark Carroll, both of whom have already secured their places in the European Championships. Brizzel will be looking to retain his 200m title at the British trials, while in-form James McIlroy will be seeking to cement his position in the British team for Madrid by claiming the 800m title. McIlroy has been in exceptional form on the European circuit in recent weeks, establishing himself as one of the fastest 800m runners in the world this winter and making a strong case for his inclusion in the Madrid lineup. Meanwhile, Cork's Mark Carroll has confirmed that he will join Cragg at the European Championships, with Carroll currently ranked third in the world 3000m rankings and Cragg occupying the top spot.

Observations of thermally driven transverse vibration of a photonic crystal waveguide (pcw) are reported. The pcw consists of two parallel nanobeams with a 240 nm vacuum gap between the beams. Models are developed and validated for the transduction of beam motion to phase and amplitude modulation of a weak optical probe propagating in a guided mode (gm) of the pcw for probe frequencies far from and near to the dielectric band edge. Since our pcw has been designed for near-field atom trapping, this research provides a foundation for evaluating possible deleterious effects of thermal motion on optical atomic traps near the surfaces of pcws. Longer term goals are to achieve strong atom-mediated links between individual phonons of vibration and single photons propagating in the gms of the pcw, thereby enabling opto-mechanics at the quantum level with atoms, photons, and phonons.

The attached spreadsheet (Jdasovic.nsf) details the implicit commodity charges derived from four distinct rates under the same rate schedule (Schedule DR), each with different effective dates. Notably, the rate schedule effective from August to September 2000 results in significantly higher commodity charges due to a lump sum reimbursement SDG&E customers will receive during this period. This reimbursement will lead to reduced electricity bills for customers.  

Excluding the lump sum reimbursement from the implicit energy component calculations, the average implicit commodity charge is 6.2 cents per kWh for June 2000 through January 2001, increasing to 7.3 cents for February 2001 through December 2001. The overall average across the entire period (June 2000 to December 2001) remains consistent at 6.9 cents per kWh. This average also holds for deliveries commencing September 1, 2000.

Understanding the emergence of unconventional superconductivity with high critical temperatures in strongly correlated two-dimensional (2D) systems remains a fundamental challenge in condensed matter physics. Ultracold 2D Fermi gases have become exemplary model systems due to their cleanliness and controllability, offering insights into the effects of strong correlations and reduced dimensionality. However, direct confirmation of superfluidity within these systems had been elusive. In our study, we provide evidence of superfluidity in an ultracold 2D Fermi gas. This is achieved by introducing a periodic potential through the system and noting an absence of dissipation below a certain critical velocity, \( v_{\rm c} \). We have quantified \( v_{\rm c} \) across varying interaction strengths and observed a peak within the transitional zone between bosonic and fermionic superfluidity. Our findings affirm that ultracold Fermi gases serve as a potent medium for exploring how reduced dimensionality impacts strongly correlated superfluids.

Palms sweaty, heart racing, stomach in knots. You can't cry for help. Not only is your throat too tight to breathe, but it'd be so embarrassing. But at some point we've all had to communicate in front of people, so you have to try and overcome it.to start, understand what stage fright is. Humans, social animals that we are, are wired to worry about reputation. Public speaking can threaten it. Before a speech, you fret, "what if people think i'm awful and i'm an idiot?"

Causal inference from observational data is a primary goal of numerous health and social science data analyses. Traditionally, academic statisticians have often viewed analyses with causal objectives skeptically. However, the emergence of "data science" offers a pivotal chance to redefine data analysis, seamlessly integrating causal inference from observational data. As others have done, we categorize the contributions of data science into three main tasks: description, prediction, and counterfactual prediction (encompassing causal inference). Clearly defining these tasks is essential for discussing the requisite data, assumptions, and analytics needed for each. We assert that inadequately highlighting the role of domain-specific expert knowledge in data analysis contributes significantly to widespread misconceptions about data science.

The third installment in the trilogy, I will address the negative aspects first. I strongly dislike pre-qualifications, as it is a step backward in storytelling, rather than forward progress. Although it does provide more story depth, the combat mechanics feel sloppy and lack the finesse of Arkham City's combat, which was a major aspect of its success. As of the time of writing this review (October 26, 2013), a game-breaking bug exists that prevents a side mission from being completed (the main story remains unaffected). Furthermore, some gadgets are inconveniently located at a ridiculous hotkey, requiring an arduous journey across the keyboard to access them. Although this may seem trivial, it could potentially disrupt the flow of combat and even break a combo in the heat of the moment.

**My Playthrough Summary (Current at Review Time):**  
- **Duration:** 8.6 hours to completion  
- **Collectibles:** 10/25 discovered  
- **Achievements:** 0/15 unlocked  

**Highlights:**  
- **Visuals:** Striking and immersive throughout, though the final ice cave level fell short of the earlier aesthetic brilliance.  
- **Story:** While the narrative itself was underwhelming, the post-credits ending twist offered a redeeming payoff that justified the time invested.  

**Critiques:**  
- **Final Level Design:** The ice caves lacked visual appeal, breaking the game's otherwise cohesive atmosphere.  
- **Gameplay Difficulty Spike:** The last level's abrupt increase in challenge led to repetitive trial-and-error segments, detracting from enjoyment.  
- **Achievement Balance:** Excessively demanding—13 of the 15 achievements remain rare, with under 2.1% of players attaining them, suggesting poor tuning for player satisfaction.

The initial shock of returning from practicum has finally begun to dissipate, and it's only now, in the second week, that the full extent of the challenges we faced is becoming clear. It's alarming to realize that post-practicum trauma is a widespread issue that has been overlooked until now. Personally, I was deeply affected, but a meeting with the course coordinator provided an opportunity for me to share my concerns and, in doing so, potentially spare another student from a similarly inadequate practicum experience. A chance encounter with a classmate who had also struggled at her assigned school further underscored the need for action. She was determined to warn the student scheduled to attend her school in the upcoming practicum, but I cautioned her that it would be more productive to address our concerns with the administration first.

Ordinary differential equation (ode) is a mathematical model used in many application areas such as climatology, bioinformatics, and chemical engineering with its intuitive appeal to modeling. Despite ode's wide usage in modeling, frequent absence of their analytic solutions makes it difficult to estimate ode parameters from the data, especially when the model has lots of variables and parameters. This paper proposes a bayesian ode parameter estimating algorithm which is fast and accurate even for models with many parameters. The proposed method approximates an ode model with a state-space model based on equations of a numeric solver. It allows fast estimation by avoiding computations of a whole numerical solution in the likelihood. The posterior is obtained by a variational bayes method, more specifically, the approximate riemannian conjugate gradient method (honkela et al.

"to do two things at once is to do neither." it's a great smackdown of multitasking, isn't it, often attributed to the roman writer publilius syrus, although you know how these things are, he probably never said it. What i'm interested in, though, is — is it true? i mean, it's obviously true for emailing at the dinner table or texting while driving or possibly for live tweeting at ted talk, as well. But i'd like to argue that for an important kind of activity, doing two things at once — or three or even four — is exactly what we should be aiming for.look no further than albert einstein. In 1905, he published four remarkable scientific papers.

Hearts secured a convincing 2-1 victory over Livingston in their Scottish Cup quarter-final tie, thanks to a blistering start that saw them score two goals within the opening 10 minutes. The match got off to a dream start for the home side when Lee Miller rose above the Livingston defence to head home a Jamie McAllister free kick, expertly floated into the box, in the first minute. The set piece was awarded after Gus Bahoken clumsily fouled Miller 35 yards from goal, and Miller made the most of the opportunity, looping his header over the outstretched Colin Meldrum from 10 yards out. Hearts doubled their advantage just nine minutes later, as Saul Mikoliunas' pace down the right flank allowed him to deliver a pinpoint cross to the back post, where McAllister timed his run to perfection, meeting the ball with a clinical header that left Meldrum with no chance. Although Craig Easton pulled a goal back for the visitors with a contentious header after an hour, Hearts held firm to book their place in the next round.

Fall 1974: i was attempting for what felt like the 13th time to fight my way through the second chapter of my high school algebra book. I was not then nor am i now a math person. I hate math. I am math phobic. You get the picture. So as i struggled to make sense out of algebra the doorbell rang. I put down my book and went to open the door. No one was there. Great. I returned to my studies and just as i was finding a little glimmer of understanding buried in my sub conscious the doorbell rang again. Bam, just like that my breakthrough disappeared. I went to the door and once again no one was there. Just what a struggling math student needs, an uninvited practical joker.

I'm grateful I brought a bag of eggs with me, just in case. As a result, Connor had a fantastic time searching for eggs throughout the park. The following morning, when he woke up, he was ecstatic to discover that the Easter bunny had indeed arrived. There's nothing more heartwarming than witnessing the excitement on a child's face. Following the egg hunt, we attended church, acknowledging that while the Easter bunny may be a fun and whimsical tradition, we prioritize instilling the true meaning of the holiday in our children.
