After graduating from a reputable liberal arts university, I didn't initially make a living from music. For about five years, my day job was quite unconventional. I was a self-employed living statue known as the Eight-Foot Bride. I love sharing this part of my life because it always piques curiosity about the real lives of street performers. On a whim, I decided to paint myself white, stood poised on a box, and placed a hat or can at my feet. When someone dropped money, I responded by offering them a flower along with intense eye contact. If they declined the flower, I would give a wistful, longing gesture as they moved on.

Ranking algorithms are integral to the functioning of online platforms, including search engines and recommendation systems. In this study, we explore an unexpected outcome of popularity-based rankings: the fewer the number of items that signify a particular indicator, the greater the proportion of total traffic they tend to garner. This phenomenon, known as the few-get-richer effect, becomes evident in scenarios where there are limited distinct categories of items (for instance, news outlets with left-leaning and right-leaning biases) and items are ranked by their popularity. Through analytical methods, we demonstrate that this effect is pronounced when users predominantly click on items that top the rankings and exhibit diverse preferences for the categories. We further examine the effect's intensity under varying assumptions about the environment and human behavior through simulations. Additionally, we validate our hypotheses via an online experiment involving human participants. Our results offer significant insights into the dissemination of misinformation.

The rate schedule, effective from August 2000 through September 2000, yields a significantly larger commodity charge, as SDG&E customers will receive a lump-sum reimbursement from SDG&E over those two months. SDG&E customers will expect lower electric bills. Assuming that the lump-sum reimbursement is not included in the implicit energy component, the average rate for June 2000 through January 2001 is 6.2 cents per kilowatt-hour, and the implicit commodity average from February 2001 through December 2001 is 7.3 cents per kilowatt-hour. The average implicit commodity rate from June 2000 through December 2001 is 6.9 cents per kilowatt-hour for deliveries starting September 1, 2000, the average would still be 6.9 cents per kilowatt-hour.

In a last-ditch effort to salvage its operations, Russian oil and gas giant Yukos is set to appear in a US court, as it battles to stave off liquidation. The company is currently being dismantled by Russian authorities to settle a staggering $27 billion tax debt. In a bid to invoke international business law and block the forced sale of its prized Yuganskneftegas oil production unit, Yukos filed for bankruptcy in the US. However, the unit was ultimately sold to state-owned oil firm Rosneft for $9.4 billion, following a disrupted state auction. Yukos' lawyers now contend that the auction process contravened US bankruptcy law, and the company, along with its primary shareholders, has vowed to pursue any entity that acquires its assets through all available legal channels.

The direpack package aims to establish a set of modern statistical dimension reduction techniques into the python universe as a single, consistent package. The dimension reduction methods included resort into three categories: projection pursuit based dimension reduction, sufficient dimension reduction, and robust m estimators for dimension reduction. As a corollary, regularized regression estimators based on these reduced dimension spaces are provided as well, ranging from classical principal component regression up to sparse partial robust m regression. The package also contains a set of classical and robust pre-processing utilities, including generalized spatial signs, as well as dedicated plotting functionality and cross-validation utilities. Finally, direpack has been written consistent with the scikit-learn api, such that the estimators can flawlessly be included into (statistical and/or machine) learning pipelines in that framework.

In 1903, the president of the united states took a three-day camping trip in california’s yosemite valley. President theodore roosevelt slept in a grove of towering sequoia trees, camped in a snowstorm, and spent hours talking around the campfire with his host and guide, conservationist john muir. Roosevelt famously loved the outdoors, but muir had invited him there for more than just camping: yosemite was in danger.though yosemite became protected land in 1864, the valley was still at risk for overdevelopment in 1903. It was at the heart of a decades-old struggle to set aside land for both preservation and public use— two goals that were much easier said than done.the battle over yosemite began with the 1849 gold rush, when miners surged west, seeking gold in the sierra nevada foothills.

In a poignant Christmas message, the Cardinal Archbishop of Westminster, Cardinal Cormac Murphy-O'Connor, lamented the staggering cost of the Iraq war, suggesting that the billions of pounds spent on the conflict in Iraq and the Middle East could have been better utilized to alleviate poverty, malnutrition, and disease. Speaking on BBC Radio 4's Thought for the Day, the Cardinal reiterated his strong opposition to the war, describing it as a "terrible" waste of resources. He joined the Archbishop of Canterbury in speaking out against the conflict, which he believes has diverted funds that could have been used to make a meaningful impact in the lives of those struggling in poverty. The Cardinal urged 2005 to be a year of action, calling for a concerted effort to "make history by ending poverty and making a difference in the lives of those who need it most.

"Halloween screenwriter and producer Debra Hill, best known for her work on the 1978 horror classic Halloween, has died in Los Angeles at the age of 54. Hill, who had been suffering from cancer, co-wrote the film, which starred Jamie Lee Curtis as a babysitter terrorized by a psychopath. Directed by John Carpenter, it grossed over $60 million (£31.3 million) worldwide, a record for an independent film at the time. Hill also collaborated with Carpenter on the films Escape from New York and The Fog. Born in New Jersey, Hill began her career as a production assistant and worked her way up through the ranks, eventually becoming an assistant director and second-unit director. She was widely regarded as a pioneering woman in the film industry, breaking ground in the 1970s by taking on roles that were more commonly held by men.

This is unquestionably my all-time favorite first-person shooter game. It's not designed for those who enjoy reckless, run-and-gun tactics. The recoil and ballistics are incredibly realistic. A single bullet to the chest, neck, or head from a rifle-caliber weapon usually results in death, unless it's just a graze. Bullets drop over long distances, and maintaining accuracy requires short, controlled bursts. There's no infinite supply of ammunition—you'll end up with partially filled magazines if you consistently reload halfway through. Sprinting endlessly isn't possible either; after running for a while, your aim will falter as you catch your breath. To improve steadiness, rest your weapon on your elbows while prone or use a window sill for support when aiming. Standing accuracy is less reliable.

Last night, I completed this game and must say, the storytelling is what truly sets it apart. Although the narrative is not particularly deep or engaging, the way it is presented is exceptional. The game's atmosphere is richly detailed, with a wealth of collectibles that do an excellent job of fleshing out the world of Batman. I learned a great deal about the Caped Crusader's universe, which was a nice surprise. However, I was disappointed that only a few bosses were included in the game. Nonetheless, there is always the opportunity to improve upon this in future titles. The combat is well-balanced and challenging, but it is also on the easier side. Leveling up Batman himself was not particularly satisfying, but the gadgets and abilities you acquire during the campaign add new layers of gameplay. Exploring previously inaccessible areas was a nice sense of progression.

The traditional landline phone in your home may soon become a relic of the past. Recent research conducted by Nokia reveals that a growing number of individuals now rely solely on their mobile phones for all their call-making and receiving needs. The study indicates that over 45 million people in the United Kingdom, Germany, the United States, and South Korea exclusively use mobile phones. While many still retain their landlines due to lower call charges, the majority of participants believe that the future lies with mobile technology. The Nokia-sponsored study also highlighted that mobile and fixed-line phones serve different functions: landlines are favored for longer conversations, whereas mobile phones are preferred for shorter chats, particularly with friends. In the UK, 69% of respondents reported choosing their landline for calls as it remains more cost-effective than using a mobile phone.

In this paper, we study the landscape of the population negative log-likelihood function of gaussian mixture models with a general number of components. Due to nonconvexity, there exist multiple local minima that are not globally optimal, even when the mixture is well-separated. We show that all local minima share the same form of structure that partially identifies the component centers of the true mixture, in the sense that each local minimum involves a non-overlapping combination of fitting multiple gaussians to a single true component and fitting a single gaussian to multiple true components. Our results apply to the setting where the true mixture components satisfy a certain separation condition, and are valid even when the number of components is over-or under-specified. For gaussian mixtures with three components, we obtain sharper results in terms of the scaling with the separation between the components.

I highly recommend this experience, despite my personal lack of enthusiasm. The game's unique approach to storytelling has the potential to be emotionally resonant, but it fell flat for me. The first half relies on shock value, which I found unimpressive. Perhaps I've become desensitized to such tactics, or maybe it was simply too straightforward. The second half, which explores themes of obsession, hallucination, depression, and more, shows promise. The concept is intriguing, but the brevity of the experience (it took me just 10 minutes to complete) limits its impact. Nonetheless, I believe others may find it more affecting.

Four captains from the Six Nations have been selected to participate in the IRB Rugby Aid match on March 5. Among those joining the Northern Hemisphere squad is Ireland's Brian O'Driscoll, alongside Fabien Pelous from France, Gordon Bulloch from Scotland, and Marco Bortolami from Italy. Sir Clive Woodward is set to coach this Northern team in a tsunami relief fundraiser match at Twickenham, where they will face Rod Macqueen's Southern Hemisphere team. "I'm looking forward to collaborating with such exceptional players," Woodward expressed. This event will serve as an opportunity for Woodward to evaluate potential selections before announcing his British and Irish Lions tour squad, which will travel to New Zealand during the summer. "The game is set to be a spectacular event," he added.

Imagine if you could plug your brain into a machine that would bring you ultimate pleasure for the rest of your life. If you were given the choice to sign up for that kind of existence, would you? that's the question philosopher robert nozick posed through a thought experiment he called the experience machine. The experiment asks us to consider a world in which scientists have developed a machine that would simulate real life while guaranteeing experiences of only pleasure and never pain. The catch? you have to permanently leave reality behind, but you'll hardly know the difference. Your experiences will be indistinguishable from reality. Life's natural ups and downs will just be replaced with an endless series of ups. Sounds great, right?

A comprehensive review of the human gait was conducted by generalizing existing models in the literature, drawing from the works of Keller and Kockshenev. This hybrid model combines a metabolic energy minimization strategy with a non-linear description of the movement of the mass center's libration, aiming to replicate the behavior of the walk-run transition. The experimental data obtained for various speed regimes reveal that the perimeter of the trajectory of the mass center is a crucial factor in quantifying this dynamic process. In collaboration with the research group in biomedical engineering, basic sciences, and laboratories at the Universidad de Manuela Beltrán in Bogotá, Colombia, an experimental procedure was developed and implemented.
